writing let's talk about writing um being a writer as far as this class is concerned is not about the things perhaps you think being a writer is about all right we're going to talk about um three quick lessons today h and hopefully these will become the themes of this class for you all right number one okay I want to disabuse you of a few Notions writing is not about inspiration I said inspiration all right writing is not about inspiration okay we'll get into this more writing is not about ideas

actually does say IDE writing is not about ideas we will talk about that writing more specifically getting published is not about luck okay what is writing about writing is about skill and today I want to try and prove this to you okay let's talk let's use some metaphors for writers right this is the kind of stuff we do um how many of you when someone sits down to play piano for you how quickly can you tell if they're a good pianist or not not 35 notes 325 325 okay um basically everybody in this room can judge a

pianist's level of skill within a minute or two of play not exactly but you can know if this is someone who's good at the piano or if this is someone who's still an amateur at the piano you can probably tell if this is a master versus just someone who's pretty good okay editors published writers people who know what they're doing can do the same thing with one page of your writing in the exact same way that is why it's not about inspiration ideas or luck because in one page I can judge how

good a writer you are okay people wonder why can editors reject manuscripts or in this new age where we are sometimes bypassing others how come readers just put something down after one page when they haven't given it a real shot you guys can judge this too you've read read enough you know enough you can judge if something is going to work from you for you pretty quickly perhaps not as quickly as as most editors can but you'll know you'll read a chapter and you'll know whe if

that person is a master if they're in that Medium um grade where they've got some good things going on it's still readable but they're obviously not a master or if this is someone's um first novel they wrote when they were 12 you can tell all these things so how do you develop this skill practice practice hey somebody knows what they're doing um the reason I say it's not about inspiration inspiration and ideas and luck are all important but it's not about those things the reason I say this is um let's

say that is another metaphor um you are world star um batter uh on a baseball team when you step up to the plate to hit that ball and you connect is it inspiration ideas or luck ideas are the wrong metaphor in here but is it luck it's skill you have done that so many times that when you step up to swing you know exactly what to do sorry you guys are in the spray range I'm just warning you you're also kind of in the wacky range um this is kind of tight border so I I'll I'll I'll I'll apologize ahead um

but it's not for the for the for the hitter uh for the baseball player it's not a matter of luck when they connect okay it's a matter of having spent thousands of hours practicing how to do that and one thing that I want to encourage in you is to start looking at writing as a little bit more of a performance art okay when you sit down to WR all that skill comes into bear and if you have practiced enough your mind will figure out the problems that that you're trying to work on the page it will

figure out how to bring out the characters it will figure out how to create a plot that is really engaging it will do this all in interesting ways because it's just natural to you the same way that The Pianist sitting down to play that piece doesn't think about it how it goes we'll talk a lot in this class about the behind the scenes we'll break get down we'll say here's what writers are doing the trick to remember is almost none of the time that's a really awkward way to say that most of

the time we are not sitting there conscious neatly and thinking I need to follow Brandon's first law of magics or I need to build a tri fail cycle for this character we're not thinking that anymore than the baseball um player is thinking all right I bring my bat down down at this trajectory this exact force in order they they don't they don't think that um perhaps they have thought that on occasion but they're not thinking it at that moment so keep that in mind as we talk about how to write

books in this class I will focus on novel writing why because that's how that's what I'm good at doing um I am mediocre at short stories I'll be upfront with you about that I'm okay at novellas um if I can get to 15 or or um or 20,000 words I start to do okay I'm terrible at 5,000 words um I will try to bring in someone who can talk to you about short stories um and tell you more about them if I can um the other reason I talk primarily about novels is I'm willing to bet that

almost everyone in this room reads novels but that very few of you um read a lot of short stories let's just do raise of hands anyone here subscribe to a short story magazine all right so we've got two of you what do you subscribe to well Leading Edge Leading Edge okay yeah Leading Edge um podcastle okay yeah uhhuh um Leading Edge Leading Edge okay so um so some of the some of the podcast ones um and some of the online ones are really fun uh daily SF and things like that but it's telling that nobody in

this room subscribes to asimov's analog or um fnsf the three big science fiction fantasy magazines because young people don't um demographically uh if we were doing this 30 years ago a good half to 60% of the room would have belonged to would subscribe to one of these magazines most of you don't read a lot of short fiction so you really shouldn't be writing a lot of short fiction one of the primary questions we get as a writer I get as a writer is should I start with short fiction um because once upon a time with

all the short fiction markets that were out there starting with short fiction was the good way to break in it no longer is um it's still a good way if you're good at it but if you there's such different art forms if you spend all your time practicing with the short story and never with the novel and yet the novel is what you want to be doing uh you're doing yourself a bit of disservice um plus with the direct uh publishing methods we have nowadays where you can just directly reach an

suggest it since a good third of our time um in the lectures will be talking about science fiction fantasy elements um you you can write um you can write children's um that includes middle grade and ya um I hope you're not writing picture books or chapter books because I have no idea how to help you if you are You' really be taking Walton's class instead um but I'm going to assume that you all are writing at least a middle grade novel middle grade is um targeted at 9 to 12 year olds you know Harry

Ideas. One thing to get through your head is that ideas are usually cheap. Boy, those don't erase real well. I'll try using these guys. I mean, they do say: Dry erase." They don't say: Permanent, don't put it on anything! I should be okay, but... One thing to get over is the idea that great ideas make a great book. Well, a certain quality of idea is important. However, a great writer can take the most basic ideas and make a brilliant novel out of it. And a terrible writer will take the best ideas in the world and will turn them into something terrible. Okay?

Once in a while there is an idea like– you know, I usually refer to Jurassic Park as one of the ideas that I wish I had. You know, bring back dinosaurs and make a theme park out of them. That is a really great story scene and some are better than others. But the thing to teach you is ideas should be cheap. You shouldn't have to worry about your ideas. You shouldn't coddle your ideas and treat them as sacrosanct. You shouldn't thing I have this one novel I have been working on for so long and it's the perfect book

and I need to just get it done and then everything will be great. Instead, you should be thinking, "Boy, that book that I've been working on for ten years that I started, that is my baby, is such a big millstone hung around me. I should cast that aside, start fresh with something new, write it for a few months, and teach myself to write. And then, once I know how to write, maybe I can to do justice to that magnificent idea that I have been working on for ten years that now is turning out to be

you know, I've been churning and working on it for so long that I have even forgotten how many drafts I have done. And, you know, this was in one draft and this other thing is in another draft, and I forget that I've taken this out, and oh, it gets to be a big mess." So, for this class, you will have to cast aside anything that you have been working on before, unless you come and get specific permission from me. Someone who has a book contract has permission to work on, you know, what they have a book contract for.

Or, I guess you have an agent, but not a contract yet. <i>Yeah, not a contract yet.</i> But, for the most part, you should be taking everything– anything have written on before–you can take something you have ideas for, something you have planned– but anything you have written on before, you should cast aside and start fresh from the beginning for this class. Some of you must just be eternal students who are continuing education, who have like, you know, three hundred credits or something. And like, they must be at a PhD because otherwise why would they want so many classes. But anyway, ideas. Ideas should be cheap. You should not treat them as sacrosanct. Um, one of my favorite stories, um, you can ask, um, You can ask Jim Butcher about this. He shared it before, when he was making this argument on this forum once, before he was published. And, um, a certain other person was kind of of the opposite school of thought.

There's a school of thought that says–and don't write this down because it is wrong– "Writing is mystical." Right? Writing is about, you know, sitting and the, you know, the muse strikes. I assume not the band. <i>Laughter.</i> And the muse strikes you and something like pops out of your head like Athena. And it's just like BOOM! And then BOOK! And then, somehow, in between this, it's like you win a Pulitzer! I don't know. That's probably not what they give anyway. You know, not a Pulitzer. <i>Laughter.</i>

A national book award! There you go! And you dropped it in basketball team. <i>Laughter.</i> And it's wonderful. There's this feeling that that's what it is. And, um, I don't subscribe to this at all. Okay? I really, really don't. And that's not to say that sometimes cool inspirations don't hit you, because we all kind of go through that. You'll have times when we're like, "I'm just on fire today and I don't know why." And you're riding it and it's great and it's awesome.

But, the thing to to remember is that for every writer who's a real pro that I talk to, they have moments like that, and they have moments where it is pure drudgery. Where they sit and... and it's like chopping wood, that's how <u><u><u><u><u><u></u> puts it. You just hit and you chop the wood and you do what you know you need to do.</u></u></u></u></u> And at the end of the day in the book, I have found that the reader can't tell the difference between these two.

<i>Some quiet laughter.</i> Okay? They can't tell the difference. And that's because, through the drafting process, you make this one better and you take out all the pretentiousness of this one. Because this one had a lot of pretentiousness in it. And this one, it will probably be a little dry, but you will have some moments of inspiration where you get connections. And this prose will get a little more engaging and this one will get a little less annoying. And then they will meet in the center and have your level of writing that is pretty cool.

Okay? So, anyway, Jim Butcher's arguing with this guy. And the guys like, "No, you have to have world class ideas to be a world class writer." And Jim's like, "Okay, fine. Give me the... Give me the two worst ideas you can come up with, and I will write a book about them and it will be awesome. And so the person told him, "Okay, so you're ideas are pokemon and the Lost Roman Legion.  <i>Some quiet laughter.</i> Write about about that." So, he wrote this book called Codex Alera,

which he eventually sold and became a big, best-selling, epic fantasy series. You can go find that story on line. It's a great story and it's true. Codex Alera is a cross between pokemon and the Lost Roman Region. And Jim, if you are watching this and I am wrong, then why didn't you tell me when I asked you? I think he dodged the question when I asked him at first. I can't remember. Anyway, ideas are cheap. Okay?

You are going to write 50,000 words. 50,000 words is...oh boy, what's a page count on that? 10,000...2,000...(indistinct) 200 pages, right? Yeah. 200 pages. It's 200 is what, double-spaced Times or double-spaced Courier, something like that. Uh, so, that's it, that's a pretty hefty chunk. The reason I chose 50,000 words is because that's what NaNoWriMo encourages people to do. National Novel Writing Month where people try to write a novel in one month in November. It's really hard to do.

Umm, unless you're writing full-time. However, a lot of people can get to 20,000 words, and so I figure in this class 50,000 words is a pretty manageable goal. It really shouldn't be that hard for you. In order to do this though, you're going to have to turn off your internal editor. Okay? You're going to have to stop revising and rewriting. In a little bit, we will talk about the differences between outliners and multi-drafters. So what we're going to do is we are going to write every week,

we are going to submit 1000 words of that to our writing group - I would suggest that you don't worry that your writing group isn't going to keep up with what your doing. Because you're going to write more than your writing group can read. So, I would suggest submitting, you know, your first thousand words and then your second and then your third, and so forth - if that makes sense - even though you'll be writing, like your 25th that week you just have to get used to that idea. Conversely, if you something that's stand-alone-ish,

like new viewpoint or something, you can go ahead and submit that to your writing group. We do this over Live Journal. We do this over Live Journal because the first few years we did it over email, and people had all kinds excuses about why their email didn't get sent, or blaming their email server, or "Boy, I'm sure it got there, it just must have gone to your spam folder." So we junked all that business and now you post it on Live Journal, because the reason for Live Journal is you

can create new account really easily and it has good friend protection - meaning you have to friend somebody, you can set it so you have to friend them to see your work, that way you're not posting your work online for everyone - and it's very easy for your group, then, to see what you've posted, and to read it. They can even leave feedback on Live Journal. It's a great tool for us in this class. So, you need to, your homework for this week, - I will not force you to submit new writing

next week, okay? For next week, we'll talk about what you can submit. I'll give you a little bit of time to get going. But, your homework for next week is to set up that Live Journal account, and ADD ME. As a friend. Because otherwise I can't read your writing. Every year, I have someone forget to add me and then I sit down to read their week and it's like, "Oh, great!" You know, I'm not a friend so I can't see your writing so, I'm like trying to email you or contact

somebody who's in your group to like, copy and paste and send it to me. It's a big pain, so DO IT. Okay? Don't forget to add me. So, do please create your Live Journal name like I said on here, even if you already have a Live Journal account, because it'll help me keep track of who's in what group. All right? You'll use your group number, the year, and your first name. And then I can match you to your face, to your writing. It's so easy for me. I know you want to be Seawumpus17

or whatever it is that your name is that your online name. But please, just be John-2011-Group-4, instead, for my sake. Okay? So, you're going to join Live Journal, you're going to set up your thing, you're going to add all your friends, we're split you up into writing groups at the end of the class. And then, for next week, submit something, but it can be writing you've done already. Okay? So, you, if you've already got a chapter done on the thing you're going to write,

submit part of that. The rest of you, you can submit an outline, if you want to work an outline. You can submit a little piece of writing you've done before that you want feedback on. This is kind of your freebie week where I let you get away with stuff that I won't let you get away with other weeks. The rest of the weeks, you have to submit writing that you wrote DURING THIS CLASS this semester. Not something you wrote last semester. This semester. The reason for that is that I want to put

the boot to your head, those of you who have been submitting the same thing to creative writing classes and to writing groups over and over again for the last three years. I know there's one or two of you in this class. Stop it! Okay? Write new stuff. This what being a writer is all about. Okay? Other than that, it's finish the 50,000 words. Our final will be, you turn in the 50,000 words and you earmark 2,000 of it for me to do a critique on. And I'll give you nice, in depth critique on it.

And it should generally be the first 2,000 words, or something near / at the beginning, with a new viewpoint, or something like that. Make it, try and make it a little self-contained, not like your best 2,000 words from like chapter 47. By the way, never submit the best 2,000 words from chapter 47 to an editor. Okay? Might as well just take your favorite words randomly through out the book and submit them in a big long list and say "Look, I have a good thesaurus," because that's going to do you just as much good.

They want to see the beginning because that's what readers are going to see. Okay? Okay, any questions on what we're doing in the class? Submit 1,000 words every week to your writing group, read your writing groups submissions, do 50,000 words by the end of the year, and you will get an A, probably. Almost certainly. You might get an A- if your writing is terrible, but it you do all that, you know. I've never given lower than like a B+; in fact, honestly, someone who's done 50,000 words, all the reading, and turned it in,

I've never given lower than an A-. This is about...the goal of this class is to learn how to have good habits as a writer. If I graded you based on how likely you were to publish, then there would be two people in the - sorry, there was a spray - there would be two people in the history of the class who ended up getting A's because I have two students who got published and went on to become full-time writers. So, I can't do that, that's not really fair. So, I'm hoping that there will be more students

or that are already are more that I haven't heard of, but there are two that have gone on to become full-time writers. [grinding sound] That's kind of interesting, isn't it? I'm not going to say it's annoying, because it's kind of steam-punky. But...[laughter] All right. Subtitles by the Amara.org community

let's talk about being a um a discovery writer versus an outliner okay Discovery writers and outliners uh this is kind of getting into the idea of um I'll preface this with the concept of um of lesson two I want to teach you today lesson one this is about skill lesson two is um is about tools um again not the band um I guess the band doesn't have an S so that was kind of L pun you'll get used to those it's okay um tools different writers write differently and aside from a few general

principles there is no one way you have to do this okay now for the class I'm going to put certain restrictions on you to try and force you to use certain tools to see if they work for you at the end of the class you may very well decide that these tools don't work for you and discard them by tools I mean methods of writing um has anyone ever had a teacher um or a book you've read on writing tell you that you have to have an outline occasionally you'll get these um has you ever read B one says you can

never have an outline um I think on writing by Stephen King says never to use one um this is because people who write these books or who um who do these um sometimes they're great writers on writing by Stephen King is brilliant it's wonderful um and Stephen King is a great writer however he s says don't use the writing group and he says don't really use an outline well those are tools that he uses different writers are going to use different tools and there's no one way to write a book and this is

best manifest by um the idea of Discovery writers outliners because it's pretty Stark and I found over the years that a lot of people fall into one of these two camps and they're kind of mutually exclusive um the discovery writers Discovery writers are those writers who work best when they don't have a lot of structure this means that um if if they have if they're forced to write an outline ahead of time what happens to Discovery writers is that they tend to get bored with that book by

the time they're done with the outline because they feel like they' just already done it and so they want to move on to something else or they feel like they're really constricted and the characters have no life because their Li lives are determined more than ahead of time um George R Martin is a discovery writer um if you can't tell by how long it takes him to write books um George R Martin is a discovery writer he calls he uses the term Gardener um which is a great term for a discovery writer Discovery writers are

those who tend to grow their story now we're not talking about this um Muse mumbo jumbo over here um discovered writers are those there still going to work on their stories every day it's not going to be about inspiration as much as it well inspiration is important um but inspiration the real inspiration comes through lots of hard work and sweat and then finally something clicks if that makes sense inspiration is not staring at a wall until something clicks inspiration is sitting and working until

something Clips um and so Discovery writers do work best without an outline okay on the other side we have what we call outliners um George Martin again calls these Architects which is a wonderful term um I don't know if I spelled that right um this is why I have spell check copy Ed um so outliners outliners are those who if they sit down to write a book and there's just an empty open vastness in front of them they have no idea where to go and don't ever get going um outliners work best when they

know what their goal is they say oh I am shooting for this specific type of story and if I take this type of story and I break it down into its components then I have this step this step and this step and they essentially are building a um a story through the the small pieces the small steps it's kind of the engineer way to write a book um and outliners will therefore build an outline for themselves an outline is not like heading one subheading B yada yada an outline is a sequence of events that

you're using as a guide a map to write your book okay um our Architects tend to um tend to have pretty explosive endings that come together really well if you think of books you've read that have really Zinger endings the authors were probably Architects oron Scott C is a famous architect okay um and he tends to do a lot of outlining for his books Spends months outlining and then he says he sits down and writes the book in a couple of weeks because he's done so much pre-writing um another friend of

mine um Kevin J Anderson is a heavy outliner in fact he um the way he writes his books is he writes a very indepth outline almost as a story proposal or pitch he he's he's famous enough and um has you know enough publishing history he doesn't need to write proposals for his books he can just say hey I want to write about this and they'll let him and they'll give me money but instead he does these big proposals because it helps him write the book and then when he sits down to write his book he like

takes each sentence in the proposal it doesn't look like an outline I've seen them they're just like long um you know long proposals in Pros form but he takes each sentence and kind of expands that and takes the next sentence expands that and turns it into a book that way he's also famous for um writing um with uh dictations he actually goes out hiking takes his um his headset and dictates a scene and then has um someone transcribe it and then he um he edits on page so a lot of people tend to fall in one of these

camps um a lot of you will be in between but um you'd be surprised at how many people tend to fall into one of these camps the thing about it is you really don't know for sure which one of these are or in which situations which of these will work better for you until you try them both out in this class you can do one or the other but if you're going to be an outliner you've got one week to make your outline and that should be enough okay let's talk about kind of the foibles and folies of these two types of

writers um the Discovery writers this isn't 100% but Discovery writers tend to like to revise a lot they like to get it just right before they move on and so you'll find that Discovery writers what they'll do is they'll write a chapter and they'll be like okay that's pretty good they'll do a couple drafts on it and then they'll write the next chapter and explore where their characters are going and they'll be like oh now I know better where my book is going so they'll go

back and they'll rewrite the first chapter a couple times and then they'll rewrite the second chapter a couple times and then they'll write the third chapter and be like oh I've discovered where my book's going now and then they'll jump back and start over again with chapter one and do this over and over and over again if any of you have written you know chapter one of your book like 12 times then this might be a problem you're following I see someone laughing that you did it yeah um then

this might be your problem Discovery writers generally need um a kind of that kick to the head to keep going they need to learn to just keep going because what a discovery D writer is really doing is writing a really long outline for their book and then when you start over after you finished it your revision will usually be pretty dramatic because then you'll know where your book's going and you'll be able to to write it through and fix it really well um so Discovery writers need to

keep going most of the time uh the other thing Discovery writers have a problem with is endings because they don't know where they're going Discovery writers tend to get to like the 90% Mark of their book and then be like all right and I guess it ends um this works okay in literary fiction where the I guess it ends ending is kind of like the the main ending that people do and then it ended um but it doesn't work so well in popular fiction where people actually want you know resolution and these sorts

of things and so Discovery writers need to learn how to write endings and usually what they need to do is finish their book give it to people and brainstorm that ending so that when they do their second draft they can point toward that ending more I see you nodding a lot are you a discovery writer I think I lean yeah lean more that way um on the other side over here outliners outliners have something they call World Builder disease a lot of times World Builders disease is you love building a setting and tweaking it and coming up

with the perfect world that you're going to write and so you spend 20 years writing the perfect world and never actually get around to writing your book um this is a big problem with um with Architects another big problem with Architects is that they tend to like rip through a first draft and be like all right that's okay and then just throw it aside and start on something new this is what I did yeah that's why I wrote 13 books before I sold one I finished one I'm like hey that was fun I'll do

another one and then immediately was more excited about the next project than this project the I'm excited about the next thing um tends to be a bit of an outliner architect thing um that could fall into both um the thing is you'll probably have attributes of both of these depending on what you're doing and you'll probably use different aspects of these um for different parts of your writing um through my history as a writer I found that um I can use both tools for different situations I tend to

architect my worlds and my settings and Discovery right my characters works very well for me helps me keep my characters a little bit fresh um it helps me keep them alive rather than it feel like their life is written out of them but it also lets me have explosive endings the problem with doing this means that my characters have line out in veto over the outline and so I often have to stop and because I'm an architect I can't just let them run wild as a lot of Discovery writers would do I have to

stop and say okay they wouldn't do that I have to rebuild my outline and so I can have to go back to the outlining stage several times during the writing the book which can be kind of frustrating when you want to you know just making progress and you're like no this character wouldn't do that to the person they've become I have to rebuild my entire plot um I've Gotten Good at doing that but it it has ruined books for me early on in my career ones I didn't publish or it made certain books

take a lot longer because um I didn't quite understand this process but my children's books I completely Discover it right just for a fun exercise um if I try to do an Epic Fantasy that way it's a disaster but in 50,000 words I can Discover right a pretty decent book um that comes out the way I want it to I can't Discovery write at over 100,000 wordss and that's just what I've discovered about myself um I need that goal and I need to have that outline in order to keep multiple plot threads

6-https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pt_7wdsQTow&list=PL2FCD81A6FE4280AC&index=6&pp=iAQB

Outlining and brainstorming. We talked last week about the fact that ideas are cheap. That's not to say they're not important but you should, as you become a writer, start generating ideas pretty quickly. If you have trouble with ideas, which is actually fairly common. If you're developing a good style but you don't quite know what to write about, there are several tricks to use to jump-start you. One is the ubiquitous writing prompt. Writing prompts are okay, I give them on my podcast "Writing Excuses" because they do work for some people.

I have never found that I really like writing prompts that much, because I am searching for good conflict with which to intersect good story telling. Really, a book, in my opinion, is about an intersection of three things. At the top, you have your character, at the side you have your setting, and over here your plot. The thing that kind of drives them together with our little "super Venn diagram" thing here is conflict. Conflict is what's going to drive these stories. You've probably heard the little famous adage.

"The queen died" is not a story, but "the queen died and then the king died of grief" is a story. The reason for that being that the king dying of grief implies more conflict. It implies a question, it implies a relationship. It's a very simple way of putting it, but when I write a book, when I start a book, I am searching for intersections between character and setting that create conflict, or between plot and setting. Plot in this case, I'm talking about your story concept plot.

Plot has many definitions. You can define plot as the stuff that gets you from chapter to chapter, even from paragraph to paragraph. In this definition, it is your one sentence pitch. Lord of the Rings - This powerful ring was created that could rule the world and the only person who can destroy it is an inexperienced furry-footed Hobbit who likes to drink tea and doesn't want to walk to the land of death but has to. Your story hooks, your plot you can kind of describe in one sentence. A better one for that is probably, Frodo traverses the dangerous grounds between The Shire and Mordor.

Or Mistborn - A gang of thieves try to rob the dark lord himself. These are your capital key plot, as I sometimes put it. A capital key plot is not going to be enough usually for me. I need an intersection of these things. A gang of thieves rip off the dark lord is fine, but who are these gang of thieves and where's the inherent conflict in there for them? Why are they doing this? That's where a story starts getting generated for me, and I suggest that's where you start looking. If you're one of those that's a brilliant world-builder, of which there will be many in this room,

what you need to start doing is start developing characters or plots based around that world-building, with points of conflict. You generally want to try and put your characters as close to that conflict as possible. One of the biggest rookie mistakes in writing is making an inactive protagonist. This happens because you develop this character and you have them wander into town and see all these problems happening and then want to fix them without giving them a personal attachment to it. Or you write a teen problem novel, but the teen problem novel is about the girl's friend

and trying to help her friend through the problem. That's usually not enough direct personal involvement. It can be, if you do it right, but if you do it poorly, it comes off as an outside observer observing the story, rather than participating in it. I frequently read new pieces of writing where the first chapter is like this. The first chapter involves a main character, a viewpoint protagonist, not actually doing anything, but watching other people do cool things. This is a terrible way to start your book.

Even if your book is going to be about the Three Act Format, which I'll talk about in a little bit, which is React, Act. Being your plot movement for your character. It's one of the main ways people build it, at first the main character reacts, and then they actually act. This is known as the superhero problem. Superman would have nothing to do if bad people didn't force him to do things. This is a conundrum for writers. A lot of the protagonists we write would be happy, as Frodo was happy, sitting in their house having pleasant afternoons

and drinking tea, until the bad guys who are very active come in and force them to do something. This is why we've had this habitual problem in fiction - it's manifested in superheros a lot, but it's not limited to them - that the villians are more interesting than the heroes. This is because the villians end up acting and the heroes end up reacting. And so your chore as a writer is to devise ways that your main protagonist can be proactive even as they are reacting. This means that you want them to have passions and goals related to the setting,

related to the other characters, things that are going on that they're working on, even though maybe the large scale plot is forcing them and forcing their hand and moving them around. That's kind of a tangent, something to keep in mind, that active protagonists are extremely compelling to us. Extremely compelling. We love active protagonists. This is why, when you watch a lot of good, classic action films, Indiana Jones, the first one, is a great example of this, they will often start with a beginning which shows the main character being extraordinarily active

before they move into something big, bad has gone wrong and now you have to react to the situation for a while before you can gain control of it again. We prove how active they are, how they're capable of doing things, and then we kind of take that away from them for a while and watch them struggle back up to it. So, getting back to the original supposition here, if you are one that has trouble developing plots, it's actually pretty easy. Start brainstorming your setting. We'll talk about setting a little bit probably next week, maybe a little bit today.

Start brainstorming your setting, where is this taking place? What's interesting about it? And start placing characters at the intersection of conflict points. Don't place them observing it. If you have an interesting religion in your world put your character directly in conflict with it. Or as a member of it, directly in conflict with someone else. Inherently, as soon as you start to do that, you will begin to build a story. You'll start to resolve that in your heard or work on it, and suddenly BOOM,

you've built for yourself a way for your character to go. If that isn't working, another good way to do this is to read history books. You can find in history lots of great examples of interesting situations. One I usually talk about is marching the elephants over the Alps. Hannibal, Alps, elephants, this is a cool concept. You read about this for a while, if you go read about this situation, maybe the assassination of Saint Thomas à Becket, or things like this, you start reading about some of these cool situations in history

and you can take the core idea of that theme and built another story around it. Maybe yours isn't going to be elephants across Alps or even whatever lizard-creatures across the desert, it could be four or five steps removed. You're going to take your space convoy from this planet to this planet that has to pass through a nebula and you're going to have some of the same hardships. You start with this concept of, here is my conflict, we have to get from here to here and there is this huge obstacle in the way,

let's look in history and see what kind of complications were involved and see if I can adapt those. Works very well. You'll see people doing it all the time. George RR Martin wrote an entire book series doing this. He's a medieval historian, he took the War of the Roses, said "I'm going to do a fantasy version of War of the Roses" and you have A Game of Thrones. The long standing tradition of this to get inspiration. It's not cheating or cheap in any way because what you're doing, if you're doing it right,

is starting with that as a launch-off point, a spring board and then you're going to extrapolate. It's like, in science you have the concept of the super-saturated solution and if you drop one little crystal of something in it, the whole thing explodes. You're looking for that crystal to start building upon, and that crystal can come from lots of different places. (Someone off camera enters the room and discusses room scheduling) (Apparently the room Brandon was using was booked by another class, lol)

So, one of the other great methods of idea generation is to look to stories you have loved reading and try to boil them down to what about them you really loved. Try and get at their core. There is an issue here, you have to do this delicately. My suggestion to you is that, for example if you love Tolkien, to take a few steps further than the "it is about Elves and Dwarves that I love." That's probably not what you actually love about Tolkien and many newer writers make this mistake.

They think "ohh, it's his setting, so I'm going to write something very similar to his setting." It probably wasn't that, at it's heart, that really grabbed you. Maybe after years and years of familiarity, and playing Dungeons and Dragons, and reading books like this, that is what you love, and if that is what you love then it's fine to write that, don't not write what you love. But for most people, it's going to be the depth of world building, all of the different

songs and things that he put into it, it might be that. Or it could be the seed, completely opposite of what I talked about last week, which is the seed of the common man trying to struggle against this enormous thing that some hero should be in instead of the common man. Maybe it's that which grabbed you about Tolkien. Whatever it is, if you can boil it down and develop that and then say "I'm going to take that as my seed to start my story", you can go a completely different direction with it

and use that as the soul of your story. This works very well. If you go see a movie and you say "I love this movie because of X, Y or Z" you know, I love heist novels, I love heist stories. The Great Train Robbery by Michael Crichton is one of my favorites. The movie Sneakers, one of my favourites. And so I, for a long time, wanted to do a heist story set in the fantasy world. Taking something, a genre like that, and saying I'm going to do it set in a science fiction or fantasy world.

That's a perfect way to do this. Usually for me, one idea like that is not enough, and so the reason I didn't write Mistborn for so many years is because I was looking for a second idea to intersect it with. I suggest you try to pack more ideas rather than fewer into your book. For Mistborn, what made me want to write it was the intersection of that idea with a separate idea I'd been having which was a world where the dark lord won. If you know the heroic mono myth, Joseph Campbell and all this stuff,

that you have someone who went on the Hero's Journey and actually failed and then became this dark overlord of the world because of his failure. What if Frodo had kept the ring? What if Voldemort had killed Harry Potter? This sort of thing, intersecting with the heist novel, created Mistborn for me. Those two things ramming together was part of my story seed, and you can do this too. You can do this and build your story from that. How many of you haven't started your novel yet? A couple of you? Not very many, that's good.

If you haven't started your novel yet, pick something like this and look for a good area of conflict in it and just start writing. I don't want you to fall too far behind, you really need to just get on this and explore the concept. (Audience question: Can you give an example of conflict between Plot and Setting?) Sure, in a lot of ways Mistborn has this. What I'm looking for is these friction points, and conflict between them doesn't necessarily mean they're in conflict with each other.

When they rub together, they create friction. For me, the idea was, I created this world where the dark lord had won, and so then I built on this horrible setting where ash falls from the sky and things like that and this is an intersection between Plot and Setting that creates a conflict in the world. If that makes sense, and so that's what I was looking for. But another way to do it would be to say, something along the lines of, I want to become the greatest swordsmen in the world and then devise something about your setting that says "no, you can't do that".

Maybe it's a caste system, your setting involves this rigorous caste system and the person belongs to a caste that cannot learn to use a sword. You've created conflict between your character and setting right there. Your plot then can be the story of the sword championships, plus conflict built around the concept of people who can't compete in the sword championships are allowed to for the first time this year. Or something like that, you kind of just build friction points so that you have a story to tell.

Student: Do you use this same process for your Alcatraz novels? Brandon: No, my Alcatraz novels are free-writen books. Let's talk about other methods of building stories. Now, I believe I mentioned the Kevin J. Anderson method, which is the start and write out a really detailed description of your book and then you take that and you take it one step and you put it into viewpoint and then you take that and you take it one step further and do a more detailed outline and then finally you write the scenes, where you take kind of each paragraph and say "this is a scene"

and write your book. I've never tried that method before, but I do know many authors who use it. The method I use for the Alcatraz books is, I say is somewhat like... You guys seen that show "Whose Line is it Anyway?" It's like Whose Line is it Anyway? With the Alcatraz books, my goal with the books is, number one: They're only one viewpoint books. The number of viewpoints you're going to use magnifies the complexity of your book manifold. For new writers, I suggest try and stick to two or three viewpoints maximum.

If you're writing a young adult book, do one or two. Okay? If you're doing epic fantasy, maybe let yourself have four, but remember, something like the Wheel of Time has big, massive, epic scope. The first book was halfway through before it added a second viewpoint. Because the complexity of the world is so big that adding a lot of different viewpoints right off the bat can just make things explode for you. As an early writer I once said "I'm going to write a book and use twelve main characters

and they're all going to have their own cool plots and be doing different things." That book was a disaster. So, that's a little aside, the idea here is that if... I don't want to say don't be ambitious, because you want to be ambitious. Think about it this way. And I will get to the answer of "how do I do Alcatraz books". But if you think of it this way, you think of, let's say... Your writing skill is right here on some arbitrary scale, this is where you are.

And if you shoot for a book that's right here, in your skill level, the book is going to turn out,,, How's it going to turn out if you shoot for one up here? Let's say you were going to write these two hypothetical books. This one turns out just wonderfully even though it's smaller in scope, and this one up here turns into a trainwreck. This one is going to be a better book even though you may look at it and say "ohh, it's less ambitious, it's less complex." Well sometimes, taking the thing which is challenging you enough to make it really interesting

yet not so grand in scope that it's going to trainwreck can actually step your skill level up a bit. It's like saying, let's say you weigh 120 pounds and you're like "I'm going to go fight in the heavyweight division because that's where the real champions are." Well no, it's... you know? There are fantastic books of small scope. "Pride and Prejudice" is a very small scope book. It's a wonderful novel. Okay? You don't look at Pride and Prejudice and say "well, yes, but it doesn't have the cast that something like Game of Thrones has."

They're doing completely different things, so even though the number of viewpoints might make this one more ambitious, this one is an equally good or better book in my opinion. Because of what it's trying to achieve and how well it achieves it. So, I'm not saying don't be ambitious, but I am saying sometimes it's best to try and look and say "I'm going to write a really great book at this level of ambition, rather than a mediocre book at this higher level of ambition, not until I get better."

I say that because of personal experience. This helped me become a better writer when I stepped it up. The books that I tried at this upper level of ambition when I was a newer writer really turned out to be horrible and I didn't learn as much as the books like "Elantris". Elantris is a smaller scope ambition book than something like "Way of Kings". And yet, there are plenty of people who like Elantris more because ambition is not the only thing that makes a good book. It's only one factor of many that can make for a good book.

So keep that in mind. So, with Alcatraz, only having one viewpoint means that I don't have to worry about the detailed plot outline as much. Our scale of complexity is going to remain relatively simple. One direct plotline that's going to be sequential and chronological. We're not going to do time jumps and things like that, which add a lot of complexity and I'm going to have only two or three set piece scenes. Meaning that we're going to be in this location, and this location, and maybe this location.

So, what that allows me to do is to write the books much more freeform. So what I do with them is that I sit down and say "okay, I'm going to give myself the grab bag brainstorming session. I'm going to take just a couple of days, I'm going to brainstorm just wacky, screwy, interesting things that make me laugh, and then I'm going to try and write a book so that they all make sense." So I'll sit down and say, okay, we're going to come up with new Smidgry talents.

If you haven't read the books, these are books about people whose magical powers are stupid things that people do, like one person is magically good at arriving late to appointments. Another person's magically good at tripping, stuff like that. I'm going to brainstorm cool lenses. Lenses are magical glasses which do cool things. Then I'm going to come up with random elements. Kind of like, in Whose Line, they draw it and say "tell a story using a tangerine and a wet noodle."

This is what I'll do, talking dinosaurs and a bad guy made of little pieces of metal. All of these random things, I'll end up taking two thirds of them and say "okay, I have to tell a story with all of these elements that feels cohesive and makes sense. Go." Ohh, and it has to be funny. And then I'll just go, chapter by chapter, and try and get these things all in, and try to hit all these points by the end of the story. It's a real fun writing method, it creates for a lot of spontaneity but it also is very silly.

So it's most suited to a middle grade type book. The reason for that being the older we get, the more we want our silliness and our seriousness separated. A middle grade kid will read an action-adventure book with a lot of silliness and still enjoy it as a serious book. It's harder to pull this off, unless you're Terry Pratchet, for adults. It really is. That's why you end up with something more like "Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy" which is really crazy, but really does not have anything in the way of compelling characters or a compelling plot.

It's just humor. In saying "just humor", I mean it's brilliant. It's great humor. But it is just humor. He's not doing a lot of things. Pratchet somehow manages to take it and have a good plot and some decent characters and humor together. He's the only one I've read that can do that, but anyway... If that's your bag then go for it, but remember that for adults generally humor is not random elements mixed together, the way it is for kids. If I write in an Alcatraz book "my penguins are on fire", they laugh.

They're like, why do you have penguins? Why are they on fire? That's hilarious! That's not generally going to be as funny for adults. Adults are going to be looking for wordplays or dramatic irony or these types of humor that are not as well suited to this random brainstorming thing that I do for Alcatraz. Okay, so, question for you guys: Have you used other methods of generating story and getting started that I haven't talked about? If so, tell me about them, and let's talk about them with the class.

I'll give you a gummy bear. Student: I once had a friend who was encouraging me to write and she asked me on Facebook to tell her a story. So as I was typing, she kept asking "tell me more", so it was basically a free write with a live audience. Brandon: Okay, considering audience, that can be very useful. I just talked about that a little with the Alcatraz books, and we will talk about the different genre divisions. In fact, maybe we'll talk about that today, because that is kind of on my list of things to talk about.

But yeah, considering audience. What else we got? I'm going to throw you a gummy bear with this hand that I didn't cough on. Student: How I wrote my novel was, it was Christmas Eve and I just thought of this really engaging dialogue and I said, I have to write this down, and then I just started asking those plot questions about what brought those characters to this point. And then went past the dialogue and wrote a novel out of that. Brandon: Awesome. I once challenged followers of me on Twitter

to do a dialogue exercise, which is, they try to write a scene with three or more characters without using dialogue tags. Instead, you differentiate each character by the way they speak. That can be, if you're having problems starting, a good way to go. Say I'm going to come up wih three characters, I'm going to come up with different ways that they talk. Not necessarily dialect, not like cockney accent dialect that you write on the page. Not Mark Twain type dialect and more, this person favours compund sentences

or this person always uses... Robert Jordan does this. Fishing metaphors. There's a woman from a fishing background and even though she's one of the most powerful women and a cultured woman, she always uses fishing metaphors. It's great, because she'll start using these complex fishing metaphors that no fisherman would ever come up with. But they're still chopping off the fish head as being a metaphor for whatever. And so that's a great way to characterize someone just through dialogue.

And you can do something like this, build your character so they each have a different way of talking. Maybe one of them uses very direct, blunt speech, and the other one always try to talk around things and placate everybody. Building a dialogue exercise that way. What else have you guys used? Student: I usually come up with a character first, decide what's wrong with them, and how they're going to fix that. Brandon: Awesome. What's wrong with them, how to fix it. That's a great way.

One of the tools I've seen people use that I like is also, what does the character want most, and why can't they have it? Student: Sometimes I take my dreams and make them make a little bit more sense. Brandon: Okay, dreams. Sure. Works fine. Student: I have younger siblings and they like to draw funny pictures and I'll ask them what is that, what does it do, where does it go and by the time we're done, we've written a short story together. Brandon: Excellent. Was there one back here?

Student: I had an original character idea in a dream, but forgot 90% of the plot, so I had to rewrite everything. Brandon: Dream journals are good. Dream journals are useful. All right, who was the last person? Student: The novel I'm writing right now, I was listening to some music and I wanted to put a story to it. Brandon: Ohh, okay. That's excellent. In fact, I do that a lot. On my points on the map, my big powerful scenes often come from listening to music as I'm working. Subtitles by the Amara.org community

Let's talk about Let's talk about third person limited over on this board here. Third person limited. Third person limited, you are in one person's head in a given scene, and you do not show things that they cannot see. You have to be careful of viewpoint errors. Because they will knock you out. They'll knock the reader out of the narrative, which means that if I'm sitting here getting a drink, and someone enters behind me, In third limited you do not say, "John entered behind him."

You say, "The door opened, and he turned to see who had entered." It's a fairly big distinction. In first person you say, "John came in." Because you're writing past, they know who it was. Though usually I would suggest just go ahead and say the door opens because this is an auditory clue It brings you more immersively into the scene and doesn't take any more words, really. Careful of errors. Third person though, what it can really do, is it can do a large cast better.

Because you're being constantly reminded of the character's viewpoint and their name as you're writing whose viewpoint you're in. It can really help you pull into a scene, sometimes it's better for a scene and setting of the immediate nature. So while you can't cheat on your infodumps, I've found that third person, that first person . . . Imagine one of those gumshoe novels. The hardboiled detective novels where they give you this awesome description of how it's raining at night.

Have any of you guys read these? You know what these are like. "The rain came outside, it was so melancholy, it felt like my soul was weeping" [Students laugh] You know what I'm saying! This gumshoe stuff, well you've got this great first person narrator, but each of those things are kinda pointing "ME ME ME ME ME ME!" Pay attention to me, as the character, as opposed to the actual scene. I found that you can do a little bit better job in third limited setting a scene.

Large cast is good, you can give that epic scope. And this is the best one for hiding things from the reader Save perhaps the third omniscient with the kind of storyteller, who will say "He didn't know this, but" Or, "There was something behind the door." You'll find out eventually what it was but, "there was something horrible behind that door." You can do that in the present narrative but it just feels cheaper. In this you can not show a viewpoint that knows, and so you can hide things.

It can work pretty well. What am I missing here? What other advantages are there? Who wants a green gummi bear? Come up with an answer and I'll give you a green one. Advantages. Why do you guys like third limited in the books that you've read it in? Alright, back there Student: You're not confined to one point of view, I suppose, so you feel more free. Brandon: Okay. You feel more free with viewpoints. One of the great things that you can do in third limited is basically impossible in most first persons, is the throwaway viewpoint.

You see this one person for a couple of pages, and they're interesting and move the scene along, but then they die or something like that Or, Tom Clancy would do this sometimes and stuff, you get the one scene from some random person's viewpoint And that's a cool thing that third limited can do much better than first. The thing you want to do with description is you want to learn somehow to be shorter, meaning briefer, which is basically the same word, but brief means something different than short.

Brief, I think that word kinda means you retain the content, but do it in fewer words. So you want to be shorter/briefer, you want to be more concrete, and you want to focus your descriptions on things that do more than one thing. Okay? But to boil it down--to be more brief--shorter and sweeter is going to have more impact.

More impact is going to hold the reader to your story better. Holding the reader to your story better makes them continue going, and makes them get more immersed in the story. Nothing is worse . . . okay there are worse things, but hyperbolicly, nothing is worse than a big boring paragraph at the beginning of a book. You want to avoid this. And there are a couple things we'll talk about here, I'm gonna write these up mostly as a reminder to me. We're gonna talk about learning curve, we wanna talk about the pyramid of abstraction,

Let's do with that. Learning curve. Learning curve is a challenge for us. What I mean by learning curve is when you pick up a book, you will have a learning curve. No matter what. You'll have to learn new names, you'll have to learn about characters, there's things you're going to have to learn and remember in order to keep track of this book. In a fantasy or science fiction book, our learning curve becomes more steep. Meaning, there's much more that people have to learn and so we have to throw it at them faster. Okay?

Learning curve is a challenge, but it's also an advantage. Most of us who read science fiction and fantasy are probably reading it because of, in part, the learning curve. It's more engaging to us, it's more imaginative cause there's more going on we have to learn. We like it, we like that aspect. But at the same time, learning curve creates a barrier of entry to the book. Because so many new names and things pile on top of a person that it can be very difficult to get going. How you manage your learning curve is a skill that's going to show off what you as a writer can do.

And how you match it to your given genre is going to be very important. For instance, YA and Middle Grade, you're going to want to shoot for less of a learning curve, a shallower learning curve. Usually the lower your learning curve the better. However, that said, there are some famous books out there that just expect you to deal with it. Anyone read Gardens of the Moon (Steven Erikson)? How's the the learning curve on that book? Student: It's pretty steep. Brandon: It's BRUTAL!

Student: I didn't figure out the magic system until the book was over. Brandon: Yeah. He throws you right into the middle of things--any time you start in medias res is going to raise your learning curve. In medias res means we're in the middle of an action sequence or something like that. [Literally: Into the middle of things] That can be good because your payoff for that is nice tension, but things are going everywhere. He just throws you in the MIDDLE of everything, and then there's like flashbacks too, and then there's like no description of how things work

You're just expected to pick it up as you go along, and his learning curve is kinda like BONK [Students laugh] It's like, run to this wall and SCRAMBLE up to it, okay then we'll be easy on you NOW IT'S RIPPING AGAIN It is a really steep learning curve. It's probably the steepest learning curve I've seen in a contemporary science fiction/ fantasy novel. Now, shallower learning curves, there are a lot of things you can do to shift this around. One thing you can do is to try and move some of your . . . oh, over here. Some of your descriptions toward being shorter and more concrete.

And trying to shift as much of the worldbuilding to as-we-go-along as possible. Don't put it all in your first paragraph, your first page or even your first chapter. Things that you don't need yet, shift them off and let us step into it so your learning curve doesn't do something like this. Unless you want it to. Other things that make learning curves less steep is the classic "start in our world and get sucked into the fantasy world". Why does this make the learning curve less steep?

Student: Cause they have to learn it too. Student: We already know our world. Brandon: You already know our world, so you start off with them only having to learn the character, and then you add the world step-by-step. But the fact that you already know our world helps with that a lot. It allows you to ease into things. This is one of the reasons why it's so prominent in middle grade is that it really helps with the learning curve. Student: The character who gets into it has to learn it as they . . .

Brandon: Yes, then you have the Watson character. The Watson character is separate but on top of the same thing. A Watson character is a character who doesn't know about what's going on, and so the narrative has a good reason to explain it to them. It's another great way to ease your learning curve. If the character's confused, you feel alright being confused. Does that make sense? If the character's like "I don't know what's happening, but all I know is I need to get over there and stay away from this thing chasing me!"

When you read the character saying that to you, you're like, "Alright! I'm on board. I know we need to get over there and not get eaten. I can deal with that." Brandon: Yeah it can be a real challenge to come up with. And this is why the "really really really different fantasy world", or science fiction world, can be extremely challenging to write. Because you can't even say, "It's as big as an orange" because they don't have oranges. And so, this is one of the reasons why fantasy books also tend to only step it so far in how different the world is. This depends on what type of book you want to write. Yeah? Brandon: That was much more common back in the day, David Eddings did a lot of that too. Nowadays we don't do it as much. I think we've found better ways to do it, and we've learned learning curve much better. You don't see the big expository prologue anymore because people realized a lot of people were just skipping them. Because they're boring. That said, most of the Pern ones, was like three paragraphs, and so was a pretty good way to do it. And you know, I have a fondness for all the old David Eddings prologues, but looking back I'm like,

"Man, those don't let you start the story at all," you get this book and you're like, "Okay I have to grind through this worldbuilding lesson, and then I can start reading my story." And people grew up reading those, then assumed they do that, but now the fiction has kinda really learned to do it better ways And you don't see those happening nearly as often. The editors are on the watch for people who can do it without doing that. So, how you do it without doing that comes into your description!

You're going to want to make it short. Briefer. And you're gonna want to focus it. What you do is you want to dole out the information as it becomes strictly relevant. Instead of, we begin our book, people are chatting then you're like BIG BLOCK OF TEXT to explain where they are! You want to shrink this down to one line as part of the conversation if you can. You can't always, but you want to try [coughs] and say "What is the really relevant information for right now? What can I move to later about this worldbuilding?"

And, is there a way I can structure and format my scene so that the rest of this can be chopped up into little chunks and be distributed throughout the first three chapters Instead of putting it right there. If you do this [putting it in one big chunk], you're going to have a hard time getting the editors past the first page. If it is anywhere in the first chapter, you're going to have a little bit of trouble, that's not to say you can't do a couple of lines here and there But you want to try and move it to a couple of lines here and there.

And this is a really tough thing to do in epic fantasy. I will tell you right now, that there's basically no getting away from doing it somewhat. But if you can learn to do these kinds of things to temper what's going on, you'll find that you hold the readers' attention more. And that's why we'll talk about the pyramid of abstraction.



15-https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eTLzzmtz9Bc&list=PL2FCD81A6FE4280AC&index=15&pp=iAQB

Pyramid of Distraction is something someone described to me in--um, in--when I was in grad school that I've absolutely fallen in love with as a way of describing this. And the Pyramid of Distraction says, that if you layer, you you--build a big, ah, foundation of concrete language Then, as you add things that are more abstract, And abstractions--we'll talk about, but as you add things that are more abstract, you will hold the reader in the scene because you will feel--they'll be like "Oh, this is this character

thinking about this, and this is part of their experience," rather than feeling, "Oh, now the narrator, the writer, has decided to give me all this information." So, your goal is like this concrete language is going to get its hooks in people, and it's going to hold them <i>there.</i> The issue is, a lot of times, these two fight with each other. Okay? And I'll talk about why that is. And so, your balancing factor is, when you add concrete-ness, you are often adding words.

If you can find a way to do it without adding words, you should almost always absolutely do it. Because it's going to make your writing that much more evocative. That much more powerful, and it's going to help with your foundation of keeping people in the scene without the abstractions popping them out. Let's talk about what an abstraction and, um, and what something concrete is. Um... Those of you who have taken the class, um, you sit this one out. Alright? I'm going to list some things at you, and I want you to tell me whether they

--how concrete, on a scale of <i>This Is Absolutely Concrete</i> and <i>This Is Absolutely Abstract.</i> Alright? We'll start with "love." Class: Abstract! Sanderson: <i>Looooove~!</i> It's what bwings us together. What about a dog? Class: Concrete. | Sanderson: Ahhhhh. There's the hook! How concrete is the word "a dog"? [Class argues.] How many--how many of you in here thought of the <i>same thing</i> when I said "a dog"?

(Silence.) Sanderson: Probably almost every one of you imagined a different dog. Okay? And in a lot of ways, we talked about love, almost all of you probably imagined the exact same thing. So in some sort of description of it, you could say that the word "a dog" and the word "love," that "dog" is the abstract one, and "love" is the concrete one. Not necessarily, 'cause I obviously think that both of them are kind of a little bit in the middle. But love is probably going to be a little higher, 'cause you

actually have to try to describe what love feels like. You're going to go into abstract language. Which is, ah--which is the issue. Describing what a dog is, is actually pretty easy to pull it down on the scale of concrete-ness. How can we do this? Class: Color, size-- Sanderson: Color, size--yeah. We can start describing the dog. And each word we add on top of this--word, word, word--is going to pull it down. Can you see why? Short-- brief, brevity--and concrete-ness, therefore, sometimes, fight with each other.

It's really easy to get very concrete with lots of words. Your goal, then, as a writer, is to come up with fewer words, the right words, in order to describe this dog in a way that cements someone into the scene, and does more than one thing. You want to describe this dog in a way that sets tone, that maybe says something about the character seeing the dog, and that also makes it concrete. This is what I talk about by doing more than one thing. If you can come up with a <i>sentence</i>

that describes that dog, that does all three of these things, then, even though you're adding words, because you're making them one sentence do so much heavy-lifting, you are pulling it down on this scale of concreteness, you are giving us information about the character, setting the tone, and maybe even, you can often give us some setting. In this one-sentence description of a dog, that'll tell you some of your info-dump work. By setting, y'know, I mean some of your--some of your

world-building, in your description of the dog. So, this is what we're looking to do. Now, people ask me--I got this question on Twitter yesterday: "How do I build my vocabulary?" This is a scary question for a writing professor. Why is that so scary for a writing professor? Student: You gotta build it so that you can read it. Sanderson: Yeah! Exactly, exactly! People who are reading--who ask that question, almost invariably are saying, "I want to go memorize longer, cooler words." That is the

<i>wrong</i> way to approach description. Okay? What you want to be able... to do is learn the right word. And having a slightly larger vocabulary will help you find that right word, but really, you want to choose the precise word. For instance, let's talk about precision. Let's say... a wooden bed. Alright? How can you make, without adding any words, the phrase "wooden" better, into something more concrete? Class: Oak. | Sanderson: An oak bed! Okay? I would even say, a log bed does a better job. Why?

Because log bed, if you've got a log bed in there, it's starting to do some of <i>this<i>. Now, we're in a lodge or something. Right?</i></i> Now we, y' know, you've got--you've got-- You've expanded your vocabulary into words you already know. That's the sort of way you should be looking to use your thesaurus. Bringing something down a step on the, um-- on the--um, the Pyramid of Distraction. By changing one word. This is what makes description work.

Student: Would it help, too, if you were to change "bed" into some other word that's... It depends. If it really is just a bed, then it should just be "a bed." Now, if you walk into this room, and you can find something to describe other than the bed, that's gonna give a better evocation of tone, character, or setting, then perhaps that is the thing to describe instead. Um, one thing to keep in mind with description is when we're talking about, y'know, those big, long

blocks of text. Let's say--y'know, there's two reason people tend to put in those big, long blocks of text. One is: big description of the setting. You walk in the room and you want to give us this huge, enormous description of this awesome room. Okay? That gets old really fast. The other reason they do it is to give a whole bunch of, um, of world-building. He was a <i>knight</i> of <i>this order</i> who believed in <i>these fundamentals<i>, and, y'know,  UMANENEMANEUHN.</i></i>

And, so speaking, that's why the gods fell and now, y'know, I mean--that's the other reason that we get these big, long paragraphs. Well, the first one, the description, you can actually, if you can do some of these heavy-lifting sentences, which we'll talk about. We'll, um, we'll try to build one here. But if you can do these heavy-lifting sentences, then you can shrink that whole paragraph down by giving us the tone. Um, when you say there's, ah, a four-post bed, in a room, we're setting the rest of the scenery.

You often don't, y'know--if you can say--you describe this bed and say, y'know, "It was trimmed with lace, it was four-post, it was made of fine oak..." You can pretty much start to extrapolate the rest of that room. It's a rich, lavish room. You don't have to then go on and tell us how rich and lavish this room is. If you can describe one thing. Y'know, if you walk in the door, and sitting right here is a pure gold wine carafe-- Is that how you say that? Yeah. Um, wine

pitcher. There's a pure gold wine pitcher sitting on a marble stand with fine, y'know, um--fine mahogany, um, trim--you can kind of fill in the rest of the scene. Y'know? You know what type of house you're in. Whereas instead, if you walk in the door and you see bullet holes in the window, and the light is flickering, and you describe those two things, everyone's gonna--you're gonna have a different view of this room, and so it's not going to be all the way down here, but it's gonna be down far enough

that, for most cases, your mood has been set, what type of house--building it is has been set, and so you can give a couple of these really concrete sentences. These really concrete descriptions, and set your tone, and your mood, and your character. So let's talk about the dog. Um, let's--let's try and do--now, this is going to add words. Okay? So, we're gonna add words. We're in a fantasy book, we're walking along, and we see a dog. How can we describe this dog? What are some of the things we can do to make it more concrete, and to

try and evoke character, or evoke setting? Student: Injured. Having a person along... | Okay, having the dog be injured kind of sets the mood, doesn't it? It's whimpering. If you describe it as a dog that "had not yet been caught and eaten," ...What does <i>that</i> say? | Student: There's predators. Yeah. If it's in the city, y'know, or something, that does say something, right? "A dog that has not yet been caught-- captured and eaten." You probably do not want to say a breed in the fantasy novel. In the science-fiction novel, you can say the breed

because it'll make it an Earth breed. So you can then do this whole thing. But sometimes you can't move it, y'know, into the--into the breed without kind of breaking... Uh, yeah. What were you going to say? What's that? Oh, yeah. Golden retriever. Mentioning the breed can do that. But you can say things--like, call it the "wolf-hound" or call it a "bird-dog," or some of these things, that um-- Usually, in fantasy, you want to try to stay away when you can't--from using the

terms that overtly come from our world. The thing is, almost all the worlds do secretly, so you're going to have to decide what your balance is here. But, y'know, calling something an "ottoman" instead of a "footstool" is probably a bad idea in a fantasy book, simply because the Ottoman Empire didn't exist and s--a lot of people know the word "ottoman" as relating to the Ottoman Empire. Um, that said, You can make an argument for saying, "Well, the whole book is in translation, anyway, and so the person who

translated it into English called it an "ottoman" because that's what we do. Um, y'know, I mean But you kind of have to watch this balance. So, how else can we describe this dog? How can we evoke character in our description of this dog? | St: Give it a sneer. | What's that? | St: If you give it some sort of expression like "a sneer--" | Okay. A sneer? Okay, yes. A snarling dog? But how can I evoke the character of the person who's seeing the dog? St: Their reaction to seeing it where it's like, it's disgusted--

St: --Or a mongrel. | Or, yeah, a mongrel. You use the word "mongrel," and it does something different or, y'know, "a disgusting dog," or, y'know, a "cute" dog, um, can give you--how, ah, what else?
