which was on the list to do during the um the prose lecture and we didn't quite get around to it it's important to what we're talking about today so the pyramid extraction uh this is something that a friend in grad school pitched to me is an idea and I really liked it I've latched on to it and the idea for the Pyramid of abstraction it's kind of like the food pyramid which apparently they don't use anymore so you kids may not even know what a food pyramid is um but the idea is that

you the bulk of your writing should be concrete concrete writing informs the foundation of your storytelling and that concrete language grounds the reader in a scene and holds them there then once a reader is grounded in a scene you can start to get abstract various things are more abstract and these kind of serve to pull the reader out of the scene and so you don't want that to happen what you want to do is have them get held in the scene by the concrete language so that drifting away doesn't happen and the

things that are abstract are often what we call Naval gazing uh that's Harriet's uh one of my editors favorite terms for it Naval gazing is the character getting introspective about themselves about their problems and when it goes wrong Naval gazing is like whining but a little bit of Naval gazing is good as it's kind of the the character being reflective um abstractions maybe any of these things where you slip into narrative and um and just explain something about the world right anything that's you know not the

immediate action or setting detail is abstract now the problem is if you do mostly this if you put the pyramid on its head you start to get some problems like um the reader doesn't feel like they're in a scene they don't attach to the character it feels like it's just you know the all these abstract ideas or World building instead of a story um it's very like the food pyramid if you can earn this with this you will have a better story now the trick is um learning to actually pull things down

the Pyramid of abstraction whenever it is reasonable to do so so your kind of natural inclination will be to go up but stronger writing usually is pulling things down to an extent for those who haven't listened to this lecture before or things like this I've got a little exercise that kind of talks about this so if you have listened to the lecture if you know about the pyramid just stay quiet for a little bit I won't let other people explore it um if you're going to say something is

abstract or concrete let me ask you where would you put that a discussion of love right where someone raise your hand where are you gonna put that I'm like yeah the characters love for another character they're sitting there pondering about it where does that go is that that sets up at the top right this is kind of one of your abstractions in fact if you just use anytime you're talking about a concept like love you're abstract um let's say you mention a dog in your story where on the Pyramid of

distraction is where are you then concrete concrete you think a dog is concrete right here let me ask you this when I said dog how many people in the room imagine the same dog I would bet there's a different dog in everyone's head and so dog certainly is more concrete than love um but it's really kind of up here it's it's pretty high um now this is this is intentional that that's kind of a trick question um everyone answers that way that's how I answered at first too and this is to

get you thinking about the idea that if you want to really set a scene you pull things down on the pyramid abstraction through detail you don't just say a dog you say you know um a wet mangy poodle with one hurt leg whimpering in a puddle okay we all imagined a very similar dog in that instance now one thing you'll notice about pulling down so that gets more more concrete right suddenly instead of saying hey dog which is this like philosophical concept you're in a scene you saw that dog you

heard it um you'll notice that pulling down on the pyramid abstraction requires words almost always what you are doing is you are trading words to pull someone down into a scene that's why the question the the um what I said earlier not the question but is it usually you want to pull people down not always your job again being a chef and not a cook is to know what effect these sorts of things have on a reader and to use the right tool at the right time as determined by you so if you want something to be more immersive

if you get if you have trouble with your readers saying you know I can't visualize what's going on or I'm getting kind of lost or it's just it's if I tell you um when I'm reading your fiction this is getting really Telly Telly is what we call that what you need to be doing is either pulling down on the pyramid extraction or cutting things out to make the tele bits more lean you kind of need to go One Direction or the other we have this thing in writing where we call which we

call show don't tell show don't tell is one of the Cardinal rules of Pros for uh storytelling um this means that an image or scene or experience in a book will be more memorable and Powerful to a reader if they experience it rather than having it simply explain to them your job as a writer is to get those things across in an experiential way the classic example of this is to instead of saying he was a nervous type man to show him sitting and tapping his foot with his arms folded he was a nervous type man takes usually

fewer words than you know he paced back and forth you know checking his watch every 30 seconds but one gives you an image of actually what type of person the the uh the character is and one just tells you yes so I'll try to do that sometimes when I'm trying to expand on what's Happening that it's of course sometimes it sounds fake you practice will make it sound less fake that's a really good question um practice will help you here and the other thing is this adage like everything in writing is really more a

matter of show when you want to and tell when you want to rather than never tell um telling can do some some good things telling can get you um to action faster you can pass time you'll see JK Rowling is actually pretty good at knowing where to show versus tell if you want somebody who's uh who's a who's who can draw a narrative like this you'll see a scene with the kids talking about things and then she'll drift into towel and let three weeks pass right um and she'll get across a couple

lines of what they discovered and then she'll show you another scene where a big Revelation is made the big Revelations don't happen in the Tells but the Tells are very important for the way she writes to stitch scene to scene to scene now Thriller Place pacing which goes really break neck usually doesn't tell anything except disguised as dialogue which a lot of people do tells this dialogue they don't ever want you to get these moments to sit back and and have this you know introspective time they want it

to always be moving you'll have to decide how you want to Pace your stories and things like this um but generally it's kind of this weird thing where oftentimes using more words can make a scene pass more quickly you'll learn how this works um you would think that all more words would make the same pass more slowly but if you're setting the scene and building the tension and and doing this sort of thing doing a good setup so that then you can do an action sequence where the reader knows where everyone is and can

keep track of them easily or a good big dialogue will actually make that scene by the end zip have zip passed way faster than if you had been left a lot of that out left the reader confused had to insert it in between here and there um and kind of crammed it down their throats okay I'm gonna talk about that next um I think but first are there any questions about this idea of the pyramid abstraction or Show versus tell is it possible to spend too much time at the bottom of the pyramid and end up

actually be an acronym so I was happy with that on here it's written p-m-r-o-s um so it's like the dyslexic version on my notes um all of these things are a way of kind of looking at who your character is what their relationship to the setting around them is and the ideas every line that you write in your story should in some way be trying to do multiple things this is a classic adage of fiction make every line do multiple things Kurt Vonnegut talked a lot about this concept one of the ways to accomplish that is to

responses differently and show how they describe the world differently you'll begin to practice how to do this with your characters the whole goal is that your very descriptions will evoke who the character is and their relationship to the world around them okay so um for this stuff I'll talk just briefly about these things um character past this doesn't mean that we need to be conveying everything about a character we don't need to know what they you know their favorite food was

when they were in second grade um that's not what this means the idea is that there will be things in the past of each character who have that have helped shape who they are and which are relevant to your story these will leave emotions on the character in the character and the things they see will remind them of things in their life and this is care writers sometimes go wrong by pretending that the character just kind of popped into existence at the beginning of the story that's not how it is for us

um everything that we see touch smell generally reminds us of things in our past this classroom reminds me of teaching this class previous years which reminds me of students from previous years and things like that um seeing the video cameras reminds me of Earl my good friend who I was roommates with for like 10 years or something like that um and so there's different things and then that makes me smell Denny's because we'd always go out to Denny's you know late at night together when we didn't

have any money and that's all we could afford um that's how the human brain works I don't want you to overload on this but when you're adding touches of things like this in it will give a personal touch to your descriptions the goal is not to go extraneously describe everything um the goal is to take the descriptions you already were going to use and make them have a flavor of characterization to them I write a friend who has a new book out as of Tuesday you should all read it it's blue screen

it's really good um Dan often points out that what works for him and I think he's really good at all of this in fact I'm not a serial killer it's like a textbook example of how to do a lot of this stuff um says that if you describe the small details of a room and let them evoke a larger setting you will have a better picture painted often than if you try to describe the whole room this is the example you know we use on Ryan it's used to sometimes a a room where the um the uh shutter is half off

on the window and there are bullet holes that simple detail tells you a whole bunch about the room so you don't need to go into the fact that it's you know in a rundown part of town um you know and that drug deals have happened here you see that and you know you see maybe you know a blood stain on the floor or you know whatever you give the little details that are Stark concrete details and let the reader paint in the rest that's the way he likes to do it so pass relationships conveying how people feel about one

another through the way that they describe the the world or the way that they engage in their dialogue in discussions with the people uh this is going to be the foundation of most stories right relationships um you're going to spend the majority of the time in most stories with two characters or three characters interacting and hopefully interesting ways the rest of it will be them maybe doing adventuresome stuff um getting this down making it kind of color every line of description this doesn't mean simply the overt things

like you know the locket reminded her of her you have her mother or things like that but it doesn't have to be as overt of that as that um if two brothers kind of have an adversary or relationship and that they're always trying to one-up one another the way that you describe how they even sit down can convey that instead of just letting that you know they sat down it could be you know he sat down and then the other brother took a book set it under and sat down so it was a little bit higher it's a funny example but you

see what I'm saying just not just doing actions but having them convey personality and relationships is going to do more with each line and that's going to help your stories be more memorable um obligations for this one I'm mostly kind of talking about the social constructs that people have that they live in the the society they live in um the fact that in Korea you don't show the bottom of your foot to people and you treat people over three even people you dislike with a certain measure of

respect their societies where the more even the more respectful you get it can often show that you dislike them more and more you can convey these things through description and dialogue without having to say this is what happens uh motivations as I talked about in my character um lesson I think that losing sight of character motivation is one of the Prime reasons that um that readers I mean that writers have characters that feel stilted this is when the character is saying something to move the plot along but the reader

does not understand why that particular character would engage in this argument oftentimes there's these moments in uh in uh new writers fiction where he thinks you protest too much right where the characters like get in an argument just to prove a point and they will belabor a point over and over again to try you know to move the plot along where meanwhile I'm sitting here thinking why does this character care so much you have not explained to me who they are enough to make me care about

why they care um and then sensibilities is kind of the unique um brain chemistry we all have where we uh we see things in interesting ways some people look at a pile of pencils and think oh I want to draw something well someone else says one of them is pointing the wrong way is that anybody here yeah um so sensibilities I also kind of put passions and uh in here likes and dislikes uh some people are dog people some people are cat people describing a dog from the eyes and a dog person might be different from describing it from a cat

person which might be different from describing it uh from the Viewpoint of an old starving person during the war right show um convey these things don't just let your descriptions be descriptions let's go ahead and do questions on character for like 15 minutes here we'll do overlapping brackets next week uh we'll go that's a plotting thing I was going to transition into that but we'll do we'll do another week on plotting next week so uh questions anything on character that you want to know or

basically anything that you want to know at all about the class you've got 15 minutes for just questions yeah do you ever find that you discover aspects about your characters you didn't know when you first conceived them as you're writing them true do I ever discover aspects about the characters that I didn't know when I confuse them as I write them um yes this happens very frequently um that is because my own kind of personal path to writing stories has been that um I am an outliner when it comes to

things like setting and plot and I'm a discovery writer with character um I found that like the big problem with outlining too much is often the characters feel wooden but the big problem with Discovery writing is that your endings are usually lame um you can fix this by as a discovery writer by doing a solid revision once you know what your ending is brainstorming with friends with Dan and Kaylin my friends who are Discovery writers they'll finish a book and say okay guys let's brainstorm how to

actually write a good ending to this book um and then you know Kalyn will usually start on page one it's extreme and write it from scratch again Dan just does a heavy revision to point toward an ending um the way being a natural outliner that I kind of try to make up for the foible love characters feeling to wooden is I found that if I planned my characters out they did feel wooden so now I come out with uh with a world I built start building a plot then I do chapters from the eyes of certain characters I've

started to plan and see who they grow into in those sample chapters then once I have them I can go back to my outline and spill this a more solid outline then I go back and I start writing and then if the character is developing in a way that I like but which does not match the outline I rebuild the outline to match who they're who they are does that make sense I asked it I did it again I always do that um now you guys are all going to notice it every time uh writers have tells by the way did I talk about this every

writer every one of you will have certain phrases and words that you end up liking more than everyone else and you will not be able to help yourself but use them do not let this destroy your ability to write once you notice what they are don't don't worry about them if it really stresses you you can do something in Microsoft Word where once you've finished the story do a search or replace with track changes on for that word replacing it with the same word what that means is when you go and

do a draft every time that word shows up it'll be highlighted and then you can choose to keep it or change it to something else um don't let it become that it's it's actually famous in writing circles that sometimes once you notice well one of your words is that it it really enhampers your ability to write because you keep writing into that word so um but anyway what's that what's up uh in Kuwait was one of mine earlier and malid jointly was another big one um yeah somebody's a few of you are

nodding if you've noticed Mala jointly in the mistborn books that was totally one of mine um yeah is there ever any danger of releasing a weaker first or second maybe third book is there a danger of releasing it weaker first second or third book yes there is but it is balanced by the danger of running out of food and never being able to you know actually make it as a writer you have to start at some point your skill is never going to be as good as it will be tomorrow and so you have to pick a point

where you're willing to release something now I do know that some authors who've gotten famous for first novels feel chained to that first novel but usually if they get famous for a first novel it's a first novel that takes off super huge and they are crying in their piles of money um and so it's a it's a really good problem to have but you know Twilight was the first novel um and uh I I don't know Stephanie but I know Christopher Paolini and he talks about Aragon I mean like I wrote this

when I was 15 right I I can see that it you know has first novel problems is way better than anything I wrote when I was 15 or that probably anyone in this room wrote when they were 15. but he looks back at that but I've asked him like would you would you give it I was like no I mean I've loved my career um but he does want to escape the idea that he was a 15 year old rider because he's in his 30s now right um so yes there is a danger but I don't think it's worth stressing about too much

um I do think it is a little bit more of a chain around your neck um in self-publishing where people don't always have um as good a support structure to kind of know when their books are getting good enough that people should be paying for them and I'm dancing around this because I don't know where that line is I I think I might have felt too early that my books were ready but by the time I was writing the launchers white sand and dragon steel and things they were good enough that my support structure said

yes these are good enough to pay for um and I would have been fine at that point launching even though it was years later that I finally sold and I'm kind of glad that those I don't release those books now I don't I don't sell those books um except for elantris which you know I did I did revise and release the other ones I are just Trump novels they sit in the trunk of the card um so I wouldn't stress this too much um I wouldn't put up your first or second novel for self-publishing

um unless you've spent a few years writing more things and learning more about the business I wouldn't write something this year and immediately release it but what do I know I mean when the cell when uh when Joe comes in who is in the trench of self-publishing you might say the exact opposite thing so ask some other authors their opinion on that one okay yeah how do you feel about writing this series yeah how do I feel about writing series versus one-off specifically as a new writer a lot of the phrase that people

banned you about that I heard a bunch is to write Standalone novels with SQL potential uh this is to keep the chat the red marks um from being too strong against you in either direction every editor or reader finishes a book that they love the number one question they will ask is where's the sequel um but a large number of people starting a story by an author they haven't heard of before want to know that it is a complete story and they're not roping themselves into getting a cliffhanger

and not being sure if this author can never even finish something um so all things being equal um you would you know in a perfect world write a hundred thousand wordish book that has a killer ending comes to a conclusion and then really sells a sequel that could happen after you've had a full experience um the reason people like Ceres is because of this learning curve thing you get to be an expert in something you've now invested a lot of time and effort into an attachment to um I think there's something strong to

be said for your first release being a standalone um because I did that and it was very good for me but um pretty much everyone that I have talked to says that your personal your first novel is garbage like you write it to write it that is it true that your first novel is garbage so conventional wisdom is that your first novel is generally terrible um I will say yes with an Asterix um there are first novels that are brilliant most of the time they get revised a whole bunch and the writer practices a

whole bunch more and then releases it but Name of the Wind and Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone were both first novels um and they're both excellent books um both with through heavy revision and most writers seem to do better by writing a book then putting that aside writing something else and then coming back and revising it then writing something else but there is a subset of writer that they write the one thing and then they learn the process through extensive and exhaustive revision and tweaking of that

one story instead that's very dangerous because a lot of people that don't break out of that get trapped in that one story and never it doesn't actually get better it just gets differenter right every time they revise it it's kind of a different story um and then they don't end up learning how to consistently write and things like that but I mean there are certain writers that that method is what created their whole career um but you know I look at um Pat whom I love as a writer and I think one of the

issues he's had is that he didn't learn to write a whole bunch of different things novel wise um and so he doesn't have the training in practice that uh someone who wrote a whole bunch of books will have but conversely what he's gained from that is a really powerful attention to detail in prose that a lot of writers who write a whole bunch of books before they get published don't have so they're straight-offs um my other questions yes the publishing world do they prefer that

or artists of any kind is inform and educate your personal creative judgment right that is basically saying you get when stuff works and you get when stuff doesn't work that's what I'm talking about with creative judgment so like that means you can learn from everything all media you consume because you watch a movie and you can sit there and you can just be loving it when it's working and you can be critiquing it when it's not you know what I mean and try to understand what makes it not work

three quarters away in the movie and it's just so dumb like the whole thing was Dumb and like this actor has this really impassioned scene we're just cracking up like yeah just go for the Academy Award why not we got the cameras you know what I mean like because it was just so like overacted and so like oh just beautiful and so we learn from that stuff right we learn from that stuff and we try not to commit those same errors in our work right we try to learn what makes your stuff work is like

to me this seems Brave and cool and if to everyone else that seems lame you're in trouble commercially right like like we're hoping that our creative judgment is informed in such a way that not everybody will agree with us because that's true for nothing right you could find anything you could name any name name your favorite movie name your favorite book and we can just hop online and watch people trashing it and having their reasons why it's not good and why it's stupid and whatever right

first thing they look for right so voice is this right voices um what you sound like as a storyteller that is your voice if you're like me that explanation is insufficient because remember they tried to teach me voice and I was like uh don't get it so think of it this way like uh you got you're with a group of friends right think of a group of friends or if you're like me you're alone in your basement and you're sitting with your friends and if each of those friends told the same

story they'd each tell it a different way and some of the friends would probably take that story that could have been funny and ruin it and make it not funny and make it kind of boring and some of those friends would just totally milk it and just make it super funny and some of those friends might take the same story and just make it kind of interesting or make themselves look smart as they tell it or whatever right like all these different ways to tell a story um we understand our conversational voice

you know what you sound like in a conversation probably like unless you're kind of a mime kind of person but like we understand that through a lifetime of practice having conversations right there's a certain way you tell a story there's a certain way you hold a conversation you are constantly writing your own dialogue and you kind of know what you sound like even if you don't know you know what you sound like you know what you sound like if you analyzed it you'd figure it out or someone could

do an impression of you if they got to know you well um that is your voice conversational voice your writing voice is what you sound like as a Storyteller it's how you tell your story what you sound like it's that music you hear of what storytelling should sound like and you hear the music a little differently than everybody else um it's it's why you could take a page of Hemingway and a page of some other dude and you could see the difference between the way they tell their story right some

some I some writers we can kind of identify by the storytelling voice that sounds kind of like Hemingway he tells his story is different than William Shakespeare you know I mean like we can hear the difference in their voices um our storytelling voice develops over time we read a lot and we write a lot as we inform our creative judgment we are also developing our storytelling voice we're figuring out what we want to sound like on paper we figure out how we tell our story what I get paid for is not the

ideas behind my story what I get paid for is how I tell them I get paid for my storytelling voice that's what a Publishers hire they hire my storytelling voice to tell the story like people are sometimes come up to me like I have this great idea and I'm sort of like that's cool but you should totally write it because I've got plenty of ideas plenty of ideas endless ideas um and and I just got to pick the good ones and then apply my storytelling voice and we developed that storytelling

voice over time we might even adopt slightly different storytelling voices to different stories we're telling right but for the most part you kind of have a fingerprint on your storytelling voice that would be recognizable even when you're trying to hide it does that make sense um so one of the most important things you will do is writer and one of the that's why I like they say I can read your first page you know if I'm going to buy your book or not kind of you know what I mean like and we're like no you haven't

had a character who was like 20. and it meant it was kind of a book without an audience it just didn't have a it was too it was too weird and too like kind of light and weird to be adult fantasy didn't have it like it could it could it could be weird but it would need more of a cynical Edge to it or something you know um and so it kind of just was it was it had what I had to do was age my character down to middle grade and then and then I was able to sell it quick um so here's here's how you differentiate

matter on the wall or whatever right um so that's so that's middle grade right we just it can still be violent characters can still die it can have lots of like the stuff I write is dark it has action it's got lots of things right lots of ingredients to what I like in a story but I don't go like super intimate on the violence I'm pretty careful with my language I treat it like what a fifth grader would or sixth grader would be like and what you would read to a fifth grade or sixth grade

whose creative judgment you share you know what I mean because if if you don't share their creative judgment if you're not in harmony with their creative judgment they are useless to you they are poison to you um they will hurt you um if you are in harmony with their creative judgment they can be invaluable to you in fact let's talk about that for a second this this topic has popped into my head a couple times it's one of the biggies perhaps the most important thing you will do as writers

or artists of any kind is inform and educate your personal creative judgment right that is basically saying you get when stuff works and you get when stuff doesn't work that's what I'm talking about with creative judgment so like that means you can learn from everything all media you consume because you watch a movie and you can sit there and you can just be loving it when it's working and you can be critiquing it when it's not you know what I mean and try to understand what makes it not work

and what makes it work why does this connect why do I care about this character why do I care about this story and why did this character why does this story and character making me laugh not on purpose right like I'm laughing in sheer mockery of like I remember like I went with Divine Comedy to watch some ski movie it was like a drama and we'd heard it was bad and so we went as a group and to just mock it just to have fun mocking it and it was really fun to mock it um and it was stuff like you get like

three quarters away in the movie and it's just so dumb like the whole thing was Dumb and like this actor has this really impassioned scene we're just cracking up like yeah just go for the Academy Award why not we got the cameras you know what I mean like because it was just so like overacted and so like oh just beautiful and so we learn from that stuff right we learn from that stuff and we try not to commit those same errors in our work right we try to learn what makes your stuff work is like

to me this seems Brave and cool and if to everyone else that seems lame you're in trouble commercially right like like we're hoping that our creative judgment is informed in such a way that not everybody will agree with us because that's true for nothing right you could find anything you could name any name name your favorite movie name your favorite book and we can just hop online and watch people trashing it and having their reasons why it's not good and why it's stupid and whatever right

like so you can't write something for everybody but you can inform your creative judgment try to have sharp creative judgment and try to hope that like-minded people will see the virtues that you see in what you create right um we inform our creative judgment as we spend a ton of time thinking hard about imaginary stuff which is like oh man embarrassing um embarrassing we think hard about imaginary stuff but but it's not embarrassing because if you get good at it you can provide for your family with

it you know what I mean so it's all right but like uh yeah no like the the creative judgment thing it cannot be taught it develops over time it's kind of like voice right did Sanderson talked voice like voice is your most important thing to have as a writer probably like when I talk to agents like when I was looking for agents right and like after I kind of got into the system and had a good agent and stuff I was like what do you mainly look for when you read a submission submission right it's always voice it's like the

first thing they look for right so voice is this right voices um what you sound like as a storyteller that is your voice if you're like me that explanation is insufficient because remember they tried to teach me voice and I was like uh don't get it so think of it this way like uh you got you're with a group of friends right think of a group of friends or if you're like me you're alone in your basement and you're sitting with your friends and if each of those friends told the same

story they'd each tell it a different way and some of the friends would probably take that story that could have been funny and ruin it and make it not funny and make it kind of boring and some of those friends would just totally milk it and just make it super funny and some of those friends might take the same story and just make it kind of interesting or make themselves look smart as they tell it or whatever right like all these different ways to tell a story um we understand our conversational voice

you know what you sound like in a conversation probably like unless you're kind of a mime kind of person but like we understand that through a lifetime of practice having conversations right there's a certain way you tell a story there's a certain way you hold a conversation you are constantly writing your own dialogue and you kind of know what you sound like even if you don't know you know what you sound like you know what you sound like if you analyzed it you'd figure it out or someone could

do an impression of you if they got to know you well um that is your voice conversational voice your writing voice is what you sound like as a Storyteller it's how you tell your story what you sound like it's that music you hear of what storytelling should sound like and you hear the music a little differently than everybody else um it's it's why you could take a page of Hemingway and a page of some other dude and you could see the difference between the way they tell their story right some

some I some writers we can kind of identify by the storytelling voice that sounds kind of like Hemingway he tells his story is different than William Shakespeare you know I mean like we can hear the difference in their voices um our storytelling voice develops over time we read a lot and we write a lot as we inform our creative judgment we are also developing our storytelling voice we're figuring out what we want to sound like on paper we figure out how we tell our story what I get paid for is not the

ideas behind my story what I get paid for is how I tell them I get paid for my storytelling voice that's what a Publishers hire they hire my storytelling voice to tell the story like people are sometimes come up to me like I have this great idea and I'm sort of like that's cool but you should totally write it because I've got plenty of ideas plenty of ideas endless ideas um and and I just got to pick the good ones and then apply my storytelling voice and we developed that storytelling

voice over time we might even adopt slightly different storytelling voices to different stories we're telling right but for the most part you kind of have a fingerprint on your storytelling voice that would be recognizable even when you're trying to hide it does that make sense um so one of the most important things you will do is writer and one of the that's why I like they say I can read your first page you know if I'm going to buy your book or not kind of you know what I mean like and we're like no you haven't

even seen the story yet they're like I do not care about the story I'm not a newbie the story is whatever it is but I want I care about your storytelling voice care about what you sound like as a Storyteller do you engage me do I read your first page and go this person knows how to tell a story they're not going to lead me on a boring conversation right it's funny Billy Wilder is a filmmaker right he made like a bunch of movies he had an incredibly successful career ranging from Double Indemnity which was

the movie that started the Noir crime um stereotypes like a like Shadows from the blinds on the desk and like voice over narration from the detective that basically started with double indemnity and so he like created something that's now a cliche but he created it um and then on the Other Extreme he did like Some Like It Hot and seven year itch some of the best comedies ever done in cinema and um his one motto was don't bore people like like I read his stuff and I'm to be that was my favorite one of my favorite

quotes any creator has ever told me was don't bore people I was like man you summed it all up right there that was pretty good and that's a matter of creative judgment it's a matter of voice like to not bore people be accomplishing something with every scene you write either it's funny or it's scary or it's Brave or it's interesting or whatever right but your every scene needs to accomplish something or you will bore people right and like even your goal shouldn't be to

board people even if it's like my character's on a long journey and I want my book to be as boring as the journey is so that it like matches you know what I mean like yeah don't do that like maybe like hint how boring it is and then like move on to a good part um like yeah that's just yeah but like let every scene accomplish something one of the hardest things we do one of the hardest applications of creative judgment as you write a story is where to start your scene and where to stop

questions and a lot of television shows because most television shows are Discovery written by necessity meaning they write a pilot they try to make it really intriguing and interesting and then they get a pick up they're like all right give us yeah give us five episodes they're like okay we need to make these really intriguing like all right full season and you're like wow but they don't know because they don't even know if they'll have a job at the end of the season and things like this and so you

get a lot of television shows where they're really really good raising questions and they are utter rubbish at answering those questions in a satisfying way you guys watch shows like this um this is a discovery writing problem and I would not say that these are bad writers I'd say that it's the medium pushes them that direction because of the way that the serial nature of what they're doing um and so they often have to like only have they only know they have they'll have to answer something that

they've been building up for for a long time but they'll only have two episodes to think of it and do it um you have a luxury over them in that you are not bound by the serial nature in the same way you're writing a Trilogy you might have some of this but you need to be able to make good on your promises and a reader is going to want to trust you now if you've already built up trust by perhaps publishing other books previously you can get away with more I can get away with more than

but let's go ahead massive failure does anyone else have some things um let me talk about this one for a minute and if I don't cover it we can have you um making things change is an important aspect of Storytelling um and if you're just feeling like oh my middle is just to get me here then the problem is you're ignoring this idea of change great plots and a lot of the great plots and stories I read at the end of every chapter you can say what changed and you can answer it something pre-dramatic

changes in most chapters either it's the characters awareness their understanding a major clue was revealed a major failure happened um things went wrong or a minor failure happened or they finally tried this thing they've been planning and it doesn't work or it does something wrong or what not having stuff change so that the reader is always feeling again like there's this progress that they're not stuck in this kind of mire a lot of times when people talk about books that they're very bored by or if they're like

I love this book but there's this section right for me and it's not even that long but I love the um the uh the Tad Williams memory sorrow and Thorne but there are a couple sections where it feels like for chapters nothing changes um you know the there there's a sequence where one of the characters is like visiting the FAE in this world and it's like it feels like forever it's probably only like two chapters right uh because this is how it happens with books but it feels like I'm like oh come on have

something move let's get back to the plot when really if things had changed if the status had changed a little bit more I would have been interested in it um I often bring up a time where I had a lot of problem with this in one of my books and I think I referenced it last time but let me bring it up again and this is um the character cez in Hero Of Ages in Hero Of Ages there's a character named today said who um it's kind of like the prep the pitch for him is a missionary for all religions he lives in

a world where all religions have been Stamped Out he's learned about them all and he goes around finding out what trying to fit religions to people like um you might put on a coat right he's like oh there was this great religion once that did this and this and this and I think you should be you should you know be a worshiper of this religion because nobody has any missionaries anymore he's like the missionary for everybody um and in the third book his some terrible things have happened in his

life and he is starting to wonder what is it that he himself actually believes right it's a it's a crisis of faith for the character who was the faith and moral center for the other characters throughout the course of the books um this sequence was really hard to write and in the first draft it was so boring so terribly boring because it's like seven chapters of cesa being like I don't believe anything anymore I'm supposed to be the moral Center but I don't have the answers and it's like

seven chapters of just you know beating your head against the wall boringness okay the problem I had is I'm like that's how depression actually feels right when you get sent into a massive depressive thing you feel like you are trapped in a world that has never changed it has never been different it will never be different and so I was accurately I feel representing what it's like to be a person who has had you know this huge bout of depression come upon them problem is it was really really bad storytelling while

also being very authentic um the way that I fixed this was with this kind of method of saying okay says it is going to try through the course of the book to find of all these religions that he has been preaching to people which one is the right one which is the actual True Religion and he is going to look at each one and if he finds some big contradiction with it or something like that he thinks that a True Religion couldn't have he will eliminate it and he's going to go through all of these

religions um what this did is is it actually let me change things every chapter um in each different setting and seeing he was in he could be mulling over one of these religions and talk about it to himself and show how he's like nope it can't be that one and casting it out which evokes the sense of Despair as you're like says Ed this isn't how religion Works um plus also uh you're not going to find what you're looking for but it's the sense of dread as things get worse and

worse so there is even though he's doing a repetitive act there is change and progress to it changing but it also kind of gives you this moment where about halfway through that you can be like I've done them all now I've had my big moment of oh what was me all this stuff that we started with before we built to instead and the sequence ended up working really well on the majority of readers who read it um highlighted is one of their favorite parts of the books is his kind of journey through these

stories so it jumped from being the worst by far aspect to the book to one of the highlights um this is kind of this idea of change promises and pacing that makes a plot work and it's interesting because it was such a small change the revision on that only took a week or so to get done but it transformed the sequence 100 percent so any other ques oh we need to do endings don't we okay any other questions about middles any other ideas on this all right um the more you practice the better you

the main character and then at the end of the book she picked one and three quarters of her audience were so mad right because three court you know the other three guys who didn't get picked were like were um those the fans of those were like this is the worst book ever this is terrible um you you know you have betrayed I've read you know how many copies are different volumes of the True Blood books and ah yeah yeah so I mean this isn't a fault of bad writing this is just something to be aware of that um it

is possible to have an ending like this there is no satisfying conclusion for everyone you're gonna have to ask yourself what is the type of story I'm playing writing what is the the Fulfillment of it for myself but at the same time you might want to be aware that it's good to foreshadow and give promises how it's going to turn out I don't think any people who read the Twilight books actually thought that the werewolf dude was gonna be who Bella ended up with even though they like it

hold out an outside hope when it happened the answer was not how dare you it was oh I knew it all along if I'd written these books the werewolf dude would have been I don't know I haven't I haven't read all the books I read the first one um but I haven't read the books but you know how I mean um foreshadowing kind of giving your indications can be helpful for this do realize that sometimes there's no way to be absolutely 100 satisfying to all of your readers but that said

learning to analyze what your promises are and the introductions of your stories and then trying to figure out how to give those conclusions at the end that will be a nice tie up of that idea very very helpful skill as a writer and is what's going to make your endings work you Discovery writers who here considers themselves a discovery writer yeah okay so it's about half a little over half you discover writers this is particularly important to get to like the last third of your book and then sit down and decide what have

been my promises from the beginning of this book why are people reading this book what is it they want to get out of it and try to make a list of those even though you're just kind of discovering your way through it you uh you outliners you probably want to go ahead and let people read the intro to your book say what kind of promises do you think I'm making what kind of a story do you expect this to be and what kind of ending you know emotional ending are you looking for very hard to get these things out of

right uh readers sometimes you're like I don't know what the end is going to be I just want to read them you want to get the tonal ending right is this going to be a big action set piece is that what you expect for my ending um give them you know the first half of your book and then ask them that um let's move on from this aspect I think I hit endings pretty hard last time we did plotting uh any other questions on plot knowing that we will have a big uh session of just q a in a couple of weeks

all right let's take a brief moment to talk a little bit more about Pros um the window pane sort of thing so when I took this class from Dave around years ago um when I was a young spry man some years ago uh there's one of the lectures he did that I I've always really liked where he talked about this idea of prose where he talked about what about Style writers get really worried about style right what is my voice is what they want to know what is my voice and I think this has fueled a little bit by writing

books and by writing classes and literature classes in particular well where the professor will talk about an author's voice capital V they say it like a professor the voice of the character um and things like this like not the character the voice of the author not character voices but your voice and writers get really hung up on what's going to be my style what's going to be my voice I don't think you should worry about this um I think that in general this is going to be an amalgamation of what you have

read and what you like in writing as you practice you will come to have your own voice to the point that it will almost be frustrating to you because you will fall into doing certain things over and over again um that are Hallmarks of your voice and it'll get a little bit frustrating oh I promised I'd tell a story about Charlotte I'll tell that in a minute um so what's that promises promises I'll tell I'll tell that it's a fun story um so um what's that yeah this one will be fulfilled I gotta

tell the story so it's so fun um it's it anyway but back here to this don't stress voice at least your voice it will come naturally do ask yourself what styles of Pros do you like why do you like them and how do they enhance the story did I talk to you guys about Orwell I don't think I did so when Dave taught this class he talked a little bit about this idea George Orwell he is the first one that I know of that described prose as the pain of glass through which you experience the story and George Orwell

said what he likes to do is he likes to make the pros as translucent as possible so you can see through it to the action happening on the other side some writers conversely like to write like they're creating a stained glass window and when you look through the pros you can see the story on the other side but if you step back and look at the stained glass window you're like wow this thing is beautiful in its own right it kind of distracts from the story but that's okay because oftentimes the

story matches it very well uh the big um people in fantasy who do this guy Gabrielle Kay in his recent books is really good at it um Gene wolf is very good at it Ursula Le Guin can turn a phrase um Pat rothfuss is the big fantasy epic fantasy writer where the prose is more turns into more stained glass than it does to window pane I prefer window pain um I prefer to try to eliminate most of the stuff about the pros that will draw attention to itself so that you enjoy the stories that's happening on the

other side you're gonna have to make this decision for yourself you're gonna have to read different books and be like oh do I care how much this is drawing attention to itself how much it's not it's really up to you let's save dialogue I see a few people who are like uh Brandon it's the end of the semester we're so tired I'll tell my funny story and then we'll be done um we'll do dialogue next time or one of the other times so I'm here in um Utah going to a

in progress I've been trying to find a way to kind of talk about what I think makes good dialogue because it's a hard one as if with a lot of things in writing it really depends on what you're trying to do but these are things to keep in mind and I'm going to go down these one at a time uh the first one is is motivation m is motive um so I would say the number one cause of stilted dialogue when I read it in a newer writer writing is because they are throwing character motive out the window

and are letting the plot Necessities Drive the story through the dialogue instead for instance they have heard well you want to show versus tell when when it comes to your world building and I've also heard that a Watson character a character who can have things explained to them is a great sort of character and so I'll put my world building in dialogue which is not a bad way to go honestly um or you're but then they dump it into a big long explanation of World building that's just as boring

as if you had put it into narrative it doesn't really achieve what you want to do because it bores everyone silly and more importantly often times there's no reason in that moment for the character to be asking for this information or if there is reason like what the heck is going on you know monsters are falling from the sky and they're they're eating everything the person exp the it could explain to them would probably say we don't have time to explain this monsters are trying to eat us but in a lot of

stories they'll be like let me tell you what's going on well the Monsters Are from Venus and the v um yeah and the motive gets lost in the plot necessity um good dialogue in my opinion is dialogue where the writer understands why each character is coming to this dialogue what they're bringing to it and keeps attention on what the character wants in life why they're talking the way that they're talking um the I is individuality um and I I kind of rank these in descending importance so individuality

is um good dialogue I feel is dialogue often where you can tell the different characters apart even without the dialogue tags because of the way that they speak now let's dig into accents and dialect how who here likes a thick dialect and writing there are none of you usually there's one or two there usually there's a couple um yeah I get sprinkled in I don't like it being okay that's that that's not yeah um dialect is a divisive thing and you have to understand that if you write in strong

dialect a large number of readers will just skip skim over it or if the whole book is in that it'll be troubling for them um that is one way to make characters distinctive is dialect the other problem dialect is um if you're trying to write an earth dialect and you do a poor job which it's very easy to do a poor job if you aren't from the culture representing that dialect it's going to become a gross gross stereotype um so two big problems with dialect um the way that you kind of

handle this one way is to sprinkle it in uh to back off on it a little bit and use a word here and there to indicate this person is speaking in a DI dialect uh someone who does this is uh Gambit from the X-Men Comics um Gambit depending on who's writing Gambit sometimes has a very thick dialect sometimes it's just sprinkled through and you're like oh yeah he's speaking with aasan accent um because of this occasional word here and there uh every you know um they're not putting an apostrophe every time he

shortens a a word some writers do um but a lot of writers don't so you can approach this in many different ways I'm more on the kind of sprinkle it in uh less is more hint at the dialect instead of Go full bore into it but different people have different um different opinions on this for instance Terry pratchet who is a master of almost all forms writing he will use a thick dialect sometimes in his books and the way he gets arounded is he has a character in the next sentence usually repeat what they said or ask a question

that implies what they said in the same way that if you can't understand iwacu or a 2D2 um then the character's context will let you understand what they're saying is there anyone who understands chewbaca or R2-D2 someone nodded their hand um and here I thought it was just random noises um but that's uh that's what Terry pret does in the we free men um is he he gets around it like this so one method is dialect what are other methods you can think of to make a character individual

you about this because a lot of writers get really heavy-handed with one this and it comes off as again a stereotype um sprinkling it in works let me ask you this someone's intelligent what are different ways that you can indicate that you have a let's not say intelligent say Highly Educated character that is not just Lots you guys have read the things big word big word big word oh this person's smart I think whether or not they use contractions a lot uh using contractions a lot that one goes overboard with a lot

that's not this there's plenty of ways to do this but what I want you to do is be thinking about this um now this one in particular is kind of dangerous because it's easy to make a character who's condescending but a lot of Highly Educated people are condescending so that might be what you want to do um I'll reference another one um I happen to know some very intelligent people um and one thing I've noticed about them is when they get together with other intelligent people their conversation

they get about topics and what they're talking about um varying sentence size and length is another great way to differentiate somebody so Rhythm and pattern Rhythm and pattern yes and I think these are going to be stronger methods than now you sometimes want to use a Shand sometimes a shorthand is really helpful in the shorthand are the things like here's a linguistic marker of which culture this person comes from or a linguistic marker for this character my experience has been that if

you overuse these they get grading on people um I've tried out a bunch of different types of these things um but they do work as a shorthand uh very well it tends to work better if you can get some of these rhythms for the main characters um someone is always worried about the implications of what someone else brings up while another person is never really thinks through the ramifications of what they say these sorts of things will help you make your characters individual in ways that don't

written go to forums um go listen to them listen to interviews take notes and if you're very worried about it the best thing you can do is find somebody from that group who can Alpha read for you after you've done it um Mary famously in her latest book which is really good um went and she was on Antiga there it was set on Antiga where you know people are speaking speaking in a thick accent and thick dialect and she wrote it all out and then paid a writer from that culture to go rewrite all the

dialogue by the characters who are speaking in that dialect to make it work she actually you know paid her as and found an actual writer um and it turned out great so that's an extreme example but it works really well all right all right today is kind of a fire hose day because I want to catch some up on things so we're going to go off the dialogue if there are other questions we're going to go talk about agents okay all right so agents the first thing that I usually have to fight against with people in

their pre preconceptions in dealing with agents is that the depictions of agents in films um and television show are usually depicting Hollywood agents um agents for us are book agents and the majority of them are in New York and anyone who's been to New York and been to La knows that the cultures are very different in these two places and um the culture cultures of agenting are very different and so you kind of need to abandon your Hollywood view of the agent and look at a book agent which

tend to be either New Yorkers or people whove lived in New York for a long time they tend to be a lot more tur and a lot more upfront with you which I like they can also be kind of blunt okay I want to prepare you for that as many of you are westerners um New Yorkers can be kind of blunt my editor the second time I met him um after you know he bought my book it was about a year later said wow you got fat have you always been this fat did I miss it that's just a New Yorker um that's just how they are right um

he's like eat more salads I need you to live a long time because you're paying my mortgage he actually said that to me um so this is that's not my agent he's a little bit more delicate than that but this is how New Yorkers can be um I like this about the agenting community because you may have in your mind this impression of Agents is being kind of slimy I have not yet met any book agents who give that feeling to me I have met Hollywood agents that give that feeling to me um New York agents so let's back

up and talk about what an agent is so an agent's job is kind of um three-fold okay first off their job is to sell your book okay secondly their job is to negotiate and three is um advice okay um now there are many different parts to these three roles but the one that you generally are most interested in particularly as a new um writer is to sell your book what a lot of agents do is they spend a lot of time going to lunch with editors talking to the editors finding out what it is that the editors want what they're buying

right now what they're excited about um and they then try to find books that match what specific editors are interested in and match those books to those editors and exchange they take 15% all right 15% um of everything that they negotiate it used to be 10% it moved up to 15% right after I broke in right about the time I broke when I broke in Joshua my agent had been taking 10% and all his new contracts said 15 I think I was one of his first new clients that was at 15 um so they do this as a subset of this they

also sell it overseas um and handle subsidiary which means things like movie deals um selling to the uh to the audiobook or things like this right yes is the 15% the the chunk of what you're getting for your 15% on your like cover what so it's yeah it's whatever that so what generally happens is the money from the publisher comes to the agent the agent takes 15% out and sends the rest to you okay this is a point of some contention to um a small subset of writers who do not want the money going to the agent first

and in that case you can negotiate a deal with the agent and the publisher that the publisher just sends 15% directly to the agent the advantage to you of having it happen this way is that what will happen is let's say let's say take me for example I have um agreements with four different Publishers uh Emperor Souls with a small press the old alcatra books were with um Scholastic um and there's still some weird things happening there we have tour and we have Random House all the money from those

various people come into Joshua um as well as all the money from 25 foreign countries he then sends me one check and at the end of the year $110.99 form for taxes which makes taxes way easier even though my taxes are crazy still um for your average Rider what this means is if you have like five or six sources of income you only have to deal with one form but the um flip side of this and why some people don't like it is if you're worried about your agent being upfront with you which hopefully you won't ever be worried

about but there are some people are like you know what you should never put someone in the control of have handling your money and then sending on some to you that's their argument so um just be aware of that argument uh certain people talk about this online and you can get a deal where it's done a different way okay so they sell your book ideally what's going to happen is you submit to an agent they really like um your submission they pick it up then they go to all of their contacts um in New York

and they say hey I've got this great book um would you be interested in reading it and they give it to like 12 of them that's what you would hope right 12 or you know whatever the best uh Publishers these Publishers read it come back to the agent and say yeah I like this and then a certain number of them are interested and then the agent handles negotiating an auction if it goes there and kind of counsels you about which of these deals they think is best though you make the final call on

from America to other countries than it is for people who want to break into America if it's originally another language um and this money was what I lived on my first deal with t was for um was $10,000 uh for a book right which is pretty good um you know uh it's it's a little higher than the average Joshua they offered me five Joshua argued it up to 10 and then they paid me that 5,000 on signing and 5,000 on like publication so the first year I was published I got like five grand to live on right um it

actually it's 10 grand because it was a two book deal so I had 10 grand my first year and then the second year was five grand because I turned in the first of the books and the third year I turned in the second of the books so my income from tour was 10 grand five grand five grand for the first three years of my life of my career but Joshua took it and sold it in multitude of other countries and so when your total income for the year is five grand from you know from the US publisher and he gets three deals

will and it's pretty easy for an agent to argue an initial offer up to about double what they offer going higher than double usually requires multiple Publishers to be interested which means they can also do an auction anytime you hear of a really big Advance um from a firsttime writer in New York it is because there has been an auction this means that multiple Publishers want the book and they are all bidding Higher and Higher and sometimes bidding too much uh to get that book it's it's basically the only way

you get advances that are huge for new writers uh very hard to do on your own because most Publishers will not work with a writer directly on an auction that's just part of the industry they don't want you submitting the books to multiple people the agent they let do that the author they don't want to do it uh if you can still get away with it you can still do it but they're not probably going to let you auction it yourself um so and you probably wouldn't want to because you would not get as good a deal

the agent should make you money if they're doing their job you will get more money despite paying them 15% um the other thing that they can do is they will argue your royalties as we spoke last time royalties are kind of set it's much there's much less wiggle room here than there are in advances um for instance like Joshua has gotten like my paperback royalties from like 6% to 8% I think or may you know it's something like that the hardback royalty has never changed but some of the break points on the on

the royalties of change like it's like you know instead of on the first 75,000 Brandon getting this percentage it's this lower percentage on the first 50,000 and then after that it goes to the higher percentage right royalties are are there's a lot less wiggle room in um where there is a lot of wiggle room are the subsidiary rights almost every contract I've ever seen from a publisher offering it has tried to take all the rights possible including all future rights in any format that it might exist um they

actually do this uh and most agents say well you can't have foreign rights you can only have what we call you know North American English or World English English is split into two North American which includes the Philippines um yes uh which is what you would expect it to be except the Phil plus the Philippines um and then World English is the right to publish it in English and distributed English in every other country um in the world which mostly means the UK and Australia and um I guess we'll let the kiwis be part of

that too um but does also mean that there's some decent bookstores in like Amsterdam and Paris that sell an English language and there's um bunches in Scandinavia a lot of Scandinavians like to read in English um and then India which is kind of a growing market for English language books that would all be handled by the UK publisher usually you sell them World English sell North American to um the publisher in America now every publisher I've known wants to buy the world English at the very least the reason you

don't want them to have it is they're usually pretty bad at expl it not always but usually um you know if all things being equal you could sell it all to your publisher and they would distribute it around the world in all these different languages and be so much easier on you you don't have to handle all these different contracts and it all comes to the publisher the problem is most of this is too small potatoes or they're too disorganized um or they're buying it because for instance one time

their Book Fairs and we gave it to them for that reason um and you know Tor really likes to hold on to the um the audiobook rights if they can and so we say all right add an extra amount of money onto the advance and we'll give those to you right um but it should all be you should be arguing from the default of they get North American and nothing else and anything else they want they should add money to um and indeed they offered T offered 5,000 for my first book Joshua came in and said um we're going to do 10 for North American

that no agent in the world will let them get away with um but they try to sneak them in anyway this is kind of the you can't write any book other books for anyone else um what they do usually let the um publisher put in is what we call a u right of first refusal which is the next and then Define what you want and mine it is the next Epic Fantasy um in the cosmere Tor gets to see before anyone else right they don't get my ya they don't get my you know non cmir books but I think that's what our

our R of refusal to tour is right now they get to see it um they get to make an offer on it then we could chop it around if we wanted to um but we couldn't take less from anyone else we have to give T the chance to match um what someone else is offering if that makes sense um that's write of first rusal usually it's very standard that they'll have write of first rusal on anything in a series that you're giving to them at least the next book in the series write a first refusal on anything

can be okay if you don't plan on writing in a lot of different genres if you do you may just want to squish that one back to a very specific term my next book for X which is similar to the book you just sold them um that one's an easy one for you to put in you don't have to worry about that one too much once you've written the book and sent it to them basically your obligation is taken care of okay but they try to squeeze occasional little things in here uh the IR agent will know to watch out for that's the

only big ones I would watch out for are are non-compete um Clauses and then of course they should not be trying to buy rights to the setting you own the copy WR to the books and the world the only ones I've ever seen try to put this in are LDS Publishers um when the big war between um Covenant and desire book was happening Covenant kept losing their authors to desar book zra book would offer them more money so Kevin started sticking in these closets they're like and then we own the rights to the world

considered work for hire the contract work for hire doesn't always mean terrible contract it just means you don't know own the world okay all right last thing that an agent will do for you is career advice and book advice uh this varies widely depending on the agent a lot of Agents like to be very involved and you send them the book and they give you advice um they like to be there for you to sound ideas against the the this is a great resource a good reason to have an agent big thing you need to remember is

that they work for you you all right now they're not your employee you are their client but either way you should remember that you are in charge of your career your agent is not a manager your agent agent handles these specific things uh that is their job and you are in a partnership with them because of that however um don't let yourself get strung along by agents the horror stories of agents and this one happens way too often is that an author aspiring author sends an agent a manuscript and

even the good agents do this it's not doesn't mean they're a bad agent but even the good agents are like I like these things about it if you can do this revision um I think I'll be willing to look at it again you do the revision you send it back and they're like yeah um I think you need to change these few other things you do that you send it back and this happens remembering that it takes them like six months to get back to you on each time and so you can be with this an agent for three years where they

haven't actually picked you up and they keep constantly asking for revisions this is very natural on their part because they're like they want to make sure you know how to do revisions and they want to be presenting the best work that they can to their contacts in the publishing industry but you can't let this go on forever you can't put all your eggs in that AG's basket you have to eventually say to them I have done your revisions I feel the book is strong now is the point where I feel like you

should either we should either make come to an agreement or I should be working on something else you should always be submitting to multiple agents during that time and not just counting on this one that has started to show some interest um you should not stop writing other things and you shouldn't take their word as gospel if they send back a bunch of feedback that you think makes the book worse um I would still suggest trying out a few of the things and saying if you're right as a new writer

that's very valuable but if you try it on you're like I just do not like this then that's probably going to be a bad agent match for you even if they did accept you all right um famously my agent is pass on a few books by friends of mine that he said to them I think these are highly sellable books that I think you will have a great career with but I'm the Rong agent for them because there are revisions I would request to make the book more enjoyable to me that I think actually would make the book kind of

betray your V vision for the book um and so a good Agent should be doing that or should be asking for their visions and if you call back or write back and say I just don't think these make a good book saying all right we probably shown me a different project um maybe this is the wrong book for me but don't revise endlessly and don't revise in a way you don't think it should be this is a different relationship from your editor once you have entered into an agreement with your editor you are contractually

obliged to at least try out the things that they suggest and come to a compromise with an editor about what you're not going to change or what how you're going to change it you should both be pleased with the book when it goes depressed the agent doesn't have have to necessarily be pleased with every aspect of the book um they work for you with the editor it's almost more like you work for them you've been contracted by the publisher to license your book to them present to them

might go play basketball even twice a week or go golfing twice a week that it's your hobby that you love it they will accept that that's good for you and that that that's a legitimate use of your time but if someone is writing a book for some reason our society is like so when is this thing that is worthless going to make money right when is that novel going to get published and when are you going to go pro um we have this kind of like the Arts that we feel like they have to prove their validity by
