the stories um and you can be anywhere in between that and I seriously intend this to be my career someday and you just have to be willing to accept that it may never become that right now part of the point of this class is kind of to prove to you that it happens more often than people think it does um I have a student or two every year who get published with a major publisher right I have a student or two every year who go self-published and consistently put things out like Joe um so in this class

we've already had one person get a book deal okay um so it's not like it's not going to it it's it's impossible it's not like you know people pretend one out of a million make it no out of a class like this of people who are serious um I mean my class with Dave that there are what four of us who are pro out of 20 people so um those are pretty good odds compared to what people say you know they might be someday uh they they pretend that it's one in a million but still it's if you're looking at a career

25% OBS even if they're that good which they might not be but if 25% odds of you spend 10 years with this and you might be able one in four chance to be Pro career-wise that's a pretty big gamble so you have to be willing to take that gamble decide where you are on this scale of I love doing this you should do it because you love it and if the pro happens then great and if you really want to go pro there are lots of things you can do we've talked about in this class to really improve your odds um but

so does that make sense that's part one of your of your question is if you're getting discouraged by you're writing you have to ask yourself why you're doing it okay now writer block is something separate um because writer block can be a symptom it's kind of like having a headache if you go to the doctor and say I have a headache they're like great that narrows our list of you know a thousand different ailments to 990 possible causes um right what what is yeah where what what have you been

doing they'll go through all these other questions same thing with writer block so many things can cause writer block um and different writers will have completely different methods of getting over writer block and this is where you have to learn your psychology and try different strategies and see what gets you past writer block for me if I have writers block there are two main causes and I've only been able to figure these out over years of practice which is which number one is the one that you

probably assume it is every time you get writers block which is something is fundamentally broken with the story and I can't continue because uh I can't write this character as who they are without CH without fixing their entire character Arc um because each step I'm taking is taking me further away you know something is so broken that each step it's uh it's taking me um away from my goals so I have to stop and rebuild the story and that's really hard um and frustrating that's one out of I don't

know one out of a hundred writers blocks but it does happen you assume that it's every one of them it's not for me most writers blocks are oh I'm having trouble with the scene I'm not feeling like writing today oh I got a bad review oh you know um I said something stupid to my roommate and or significant other and they're mad at me and so it's hard to keep keep focused it's one of those many things and so you just feel cruddy about your writing that day that's most of them um for

me the best way to get beyond that is to write the seen anyway um write it poorly uh this part of writer's block is the fear that you're writing something bad the secret fear that you're a hack um is part of what's the motivation for this writer's block and really um it also it happens a lot in the early career of writers that their ability to see good writing has outstripped their ability to write it so they get discouraged about what they're creating because um you you see on the

page something you're like this is not what I imagined when I took jazz band for the first time I was a pretty good Trumpeter at that point I'd taken lessons for like eight years took jazzband could not improv I could hear an improv song and the notes would not come out the front of the horn and I was so frustrated and my teacher's like yes you need to go really reinforce your skills it's a new way of thinking it will take you years to figure this out you're like I know stories I've read

lots of great stories I know how how to write I've been trained in writing since I was in kindergarten I know how to write but storytelling through Pros is a completely different way of writing that you're going to have to rebuild you're thinking about and practice a lot and you can you can recognize it's bad so these sorts of things you just need to write it poorly first even you know if you're a professional writer and you're having a bad day you just need to write it anyway and I will tell you nine out

of 10 times that I do that the chapter doesn't suck I go back the next day I'm like oh it I was just in a funk but my skill that I prepared myself with for year of years of practice still came through and I can do a quick revision and the chapter looks just like the one that I wrote the day before when I was totally on fire um one out of 10 times it didn't but the mere fact of writing it poorly now that there's something there to fix my brain's like oh I know what you did wrong oh let's take this a

completely different direction that chapter and we'll fix the chapter so I often say that if you have a serious bout of writers block um try just having ninjas attack right just do something write that scene in some way um have ninjas attack or write it from a different Viewpoint put it in a different location do something to shake it up and make yourself excited about it have something go wrong in the chapter you weren't planning don't assume it's going to end up in the book just write

it and let your subconscious work on it that tends to work for a lot of people um students that have had writers block that come in like and I've told them to do these separate things it almost always gets them out of it um another one you can try is try a monologue by your characters explaining their um their motives why they what the they feel about the the outline that you've given to them you know do some of those free writes uh with your character viewpoints uh try moving to writing long

hand is another one that helps a lot of people if you have writer block just for that chapter write it in long hand helps you turn off your internal editor just write it out to yourself it doesn't have to be good to actually type it in the computer um stuff like that okay all right other questions hopefully ones I will answer faster than that you mentioned that we might talk about Publishers and show and Tu okay so I can talk a little bit about Publishers um and I'll try to do kind of a quicker version of this um but um I

think we'll leave Show versus tell for go research Show versus tell online I've talked about it it is just one of these fundamental things to do in writing with Pros learn how to show versus tell and it's so much easier if you can find like an exercise online where they'll say try to trim this down by 10% try it with your writing try to make it more active try to get rid of passive vers like you know highlight all the wases and to bees and things like that he was a um a man like this that highlights it you say

well how can I show that he was a man like this try some of those exercises it's one of those Hands-On things that you really have to do a lot of um Publishers so here's the thing um how many of you guys are writing teen or middle grade um so you guys I can help a lot less the reason for this is that I broke in through Epic Fantasy um not through teen I have published in teen and I started publishing in middle grade like two years after I broke in but I already had an agent and my agent

handled it and so I have much less in the trenches advice on teen when I've asked about it a lot of people say that teen does tend to be more corporate meaning that um it's a lot less easy to break in without any agent into teen and middle grade um and this kind of follows one of the fundamental rules of bad writing in that it's really hard to write a good teen book just as hard as it is to write a good adult book but because they're much shorter it's a lot easier to write a bad one um so for

instance picture books which are the easiest to write bad versions of still very hard to write a good one but you know it takes you you could probably write a bad one in an afternoon um they get so many submissions right um and Middle grades and chapter books are the same way so much easier to write a chapter book you take a few weekends you write out you know the 5,000 words um that could go in one of these short chapter books and they send them in and you're like yes but it doesn't take as long to review but you

got to remember they're only reading one page of it no matter what it is so the people who are having the the teen submissions are having just way more submissions because more people are writing them poorly um so um the what this has resulted in is that teen Publishers tend to be a bit more corporate the other big distinction here is science fiction fantasy had a very Grassroots start um a lot of writers and a lot of fans said nobody's writing the stuff that we want we will go ahead and

write it create our fan zes Publishers grew out of that all the big Publishers in science fiction um that I can think of that have a legacy that weren't just started brand new were started because some fan became a publisher to publish these books and started as a small press and so things like dah was Donald wolheim right and things like Del re was Lester Del re um and uh Tor was Tom dhy um these are started by fans basically uh no one got into science fiction because of the money that if you wanted

the money you went into other areas of publishing you went into science fiction because you like science fiction so what this means is there are a lot of Publishers in science fiction who still have this kind of tradition of Grassroots this kind of tradition of we don't need no agents you know we are fans and we like publishing fans um this also means that some of the Publishers in science fiction are a lot more open to new writers but also have um more of a chaotic approach to publishing and

we'll kind of move up uh the list um the on this side the most chaotic is tour um tour is the biggest and um the most wonderfully chaotic of them all uh when I published with t when I said I really want to be with t my agent said that's good as long as you understand one thing there is no adult supervision at tour that's what he said um he said Tom DH is the benevolent grandfather and he lets the grandchildren do whatever they want um and that is a tongue and cheek summary of what Tom has created in a

publisher that is capable of um publishing any great science fiction regardless of editorial style they can publish something by Jean wolf um that is really literary they can then go publish something might me or John schaly which is very Market friendly they can do everything in between they've got um you know they got queer fiction they've got uh they've got steampunk they've got um you know stuff that feels like a thriller that's an an action thriller they've got everything at tour anything

that you would want to publish that has a science fiction fantasy event tour will take that's different than a lot of the Publishers some of the others are like that but U many of them are not they have we only publish you know 100 books a year or fewer we publish like you know 30 or 40 we have to have a consistent editorial vision for our for our line so they feel similar that's kind of the standard way you do publishing for for a lot of these imprints so at tour um your biggest tour will take unagented submissions it will

take unsolicited submissions most of the time you look on their website they'll tell you but they are the most open to new writers but they are also the ones who get the most submissions have the most editors who are very different from one another and what they want to publish they have a wall of slush um and whether or not you um you get published by tour Can Depend entirely on if you got your book in the hands of the editor who likes the type of story you're writing instead of the one who doesn't so um if you want to

publish with tour what I recommend is Lear finding out the different names of the different editors and it changes so I don't even know them all myself um find out who they are what writers they have published recently um who they rep who they who they do and particularly what new writers they have bought and published in the last few years um learn their names try to meet them at conventions try to follow their blogs just learn who they are not so you're stalking them your job is not you know

to to go uh find them at the conventions and just flood them with uh with you know with questions for my students but it's you know go to their panels at the cons learn who they are if you find them in the p in the the rooms or the the hallway say hey if you what if you had come out recently by a new writer that I can read so I can kind of get a feel for what's selling they'll love telling you about that don't go ask them what are you looking for that's usually a bad question because what they're looking

for is something awesome and every time they they'll hum and haot at that question you ask them what they what they recently bought what they just had come out tell me about your your authors you're really excited about particularly ones that you discovered they will always be excited to tell you about that and that goes for every publisher at every house teen included if you can find the editor and say what have you bought recently what you know what have you had come out that you discovered I

want to go read it tell me about it do your research in other words this is how you start networking um you get a you get a page in your files about the different Publishers and you start start learning who they are so that's tour very similar to tour is Bane um Bane has that same mindset um started by Jim Bane uh with half of his funding came from Tom dhy as a silent partner um because they were you know he couldn't be involved in it because of uh whatever you call it um conflict of interest because he was running a

different company um he's by the way tour has been he sold in the 90s tour to McMillan you hear about the big five or the big six that's one of the big six um big five now uh Publishers so ban is an independent publisher uh is the largest independent publisher science fiction in fantasy it is not um part of the big five conglomeration um it was started by Jim Bane who uh wanted to have books that were fun this is kind of Bane's editorial Vision that I see is kind of fun um fun you kind of have to learn

what Bane Books are um they do a lot more pulish type stuff which means High action um high amounts of uh of you know great plotting um but they do have some variety in that um by the way tour is everything um so Bane does a lot of military SF um it does a lot of kind of um fast-paced fantasy it can be epic or not but kind of fast-paced um but it also has some people uh like Eric Flint who do a little slower paced um alternate history stuff so Eric Flint is another big one um but again you kind

of have to learn this is like they want to publish stuff that feels like updated classic science fiction okay you're not going to go to Bane and find something like down and out in the Magic Kingdom by Cory drro um generally that's not something that meets their editoral Vision any editor at any publisher can find something they love and convince everyone else to get behind it and publish it that can happen but generally Bane is going to be looking for this kind of stuff um Larry Korea is a big

author of theirs David Weber is a big author of theirs um that sort of stuff Bane is smaller than all the other Publishers I will write up on here they have smaller advances um they go to hard back less quickly you have to prove yourself but they also are very approachable um and in my opinion very well run I like the editorial uh Direction at bang quite a bit so if you're happen to be writing this style of stuff or have something like it Bane is a good place to be looking Eric James Stone published with

they have the two main imprints and publish everything so this they are different Publishers but uh over here they're probably pushing toward the same editorial Direction so uh they they do the uh they do the the expanse right Isaac yeah so the expanse is um is epic Space Opera uh very fast-paced also so there you go there's your quick thing on Publishers so you refer to cons like what kind of cons will we doing good question okay so um there's General types of conventions um let's talk about the three types that

you'll run into the first is a comic con comic cons are fun but generally the worst place place to find editors okay um because there's so many people at a Comic Con and because they're focused on exhibiting to fans um to large in large scale groups the editors generally stay away or if they're there they're not on any paneling because the celebrities are on the paneling so hard to find you can sometimes go to a booth at a ComicCon if they have one most of the time they don't the Publishers do not but once in

a while they they will and you can say hey are any of your editors here um that's worth a shot the answer is probably no and or if they were we wouldn't tell you but so go to Comic Cons if you like them for fun but not the best place that leaves two types of cons and I separate them by calling them cons and conferences a conference is something like writer writer free and readers a conference is has a high ticket price to come get in and it is treated like a workshop and or pitch session um if you

go to writers for young readers or um the uh LDS story makers which I think they're just calling story makers these days uh they they will generally bring in an agent or and an editor or something like that part of your uh your fee to get in is the fact that they are doing this and then you can sign up for pitch sessions it's all very formal and you have your opportunities to talk to these people and things like that and watch um programs by them that's basically your best shot in teen is to

find the conferences right in teen and ya a lot of the Teen editors do these things they come to rer fing readers so they go to like the mai Writers Workshop or things like that and you can find them cons are like what I'm doing tomorrow which is where these grassroot science fiction things all started which are um you know fans get together for uh it's like like the univers and everything um conduit and Salt Lake these are fan run not for-profit um nerd extravaganzas um they are much smaller

they'll have like 500 people at them and the if the editors are there they are generally going for the fun of it um so these are the best best places often to meet the editors but they don't usually travel the editors don't it's not like they're coming to life news and everything like my agent came one time uh because I'm out here so you can find them randomly but more often you have to go to the bigger two which are world conon or World fantasy convention and um editors generally

they'll be a a percentage of editors there world conon there's often two or three people from tour the whole Bane crew is usually there there'll be some people from Ace um there might be someone from dah or Del re um the orbit people have been coming to those as well um I don't know the Saga people I met the Voyager or the Harper people at um at Kevin J Anderson's um Superstars writing and that's a conference rather than a con um but you know if you go to world con World fantasy you can sometimes find

them um just at parties you have to be very outgoing to do do that but one of the cool things they do is at most World cons they have a what's new from tour where each of the editors that are there get up and pitch to The Crowd the new books that they bought plus you can also usually find um a panel or two with agents and editors on them talking about being agents and editors and those are gold when I found those at conventions like you you could I could F my little black book of um of of editor names with

like okay this person said this at this thing this person said this oh this is this person's editor like I got so much information from those things but they're still you have to travel like they're expensive um are there are ways to do this without traveling so again kind of watching um which books they publish looking in the forwards of books to see which editors are edting which books is usually the author will acknowledge them um looking online at the um the agent an editor blogs the

agents stealth it a lot more at these things at the cons um at the conferences they're more upfront um because they're usually you're doing pitch sessions with them are most the attendees of these things all aspiring off um so at World con I would say that it is uh you got a mix at worldcon you'll have a bunch of the pros a lot of the pros go to worldcon to just hang out with their friends right you will have a bunch of what we call semi-pros or aspiring Pros uh kind of on the Gambit of there and so

that's like a third and then there's also like this third group um that is the people actually running the con who are just fandom they love fandom and they're like people like my editor mosha before he became an editor they just love Science Fiction and Fantasy A lot of these people were fans of all of this before the internet existed and so they threw the cons in order to get together with their friends who had similar interests um it they're like giant Facebook pages from the 50s

and they're still going right now um and so there are a lot of aspiring Pros um don't make yourself annoying if you go and you talk to an editor there like what you want to do is I would suggest if you if this is the route you want to take which is not the only route I would get together a couple friends go together share a hotel room find out the panels that are on go watch them get familiar with who they are ask them after the panel if they have a few minutes to sit down and chat ask them

maybe what they're working on and stuff like that or find your own things to talk to them about um but you know in the past I've mentioned how useful these things were in my career so the the editors will all go to world like you know 80 more of your students found me I'm like yeah that's what they're doing they're doing their job that's what they're supposed to be doing they like all right yes Anderson all your students so um but it you know it has worked before this is how Brian mlen got

published um was by going and doing these things and he found the people at orbit and just was really good at um doing what I just talked about so not the only way by far you can still submit just kind of blindly but even if you submit blindly what you want to be doing is submitting a manuscript that says you know dear um instead of dear Acquisitions editor you want to be able to say um you know dear MOA fader I know that you um publish are the editor who discovered and publishes Brandon Sanderson I love

Brandon's Works um I have you know read your blog posts about these things he doesn't blog he does on Facebook though I followed you on Facebook for a while I really like how you talk about writing I think we'd be a good match um here is my manuscript submitted as tours uh manip submission guidelines go that's what you want to be doing even if you can't go to the cons instead of just dear Acquisitions editor please find my manuscript en closed um by the way mosha uh mosha had mosha mosha is really slow

so I'll warn you about my editor I love MOS to death but since I've been published with him for 15 years I I can count the number of authors he's picked up on one hand um and he still just does only does this part-time so uh Mosa's not a good bet most people at tour are acquiring way more than mosha is but I love mosha yeah so in a conference where you're doing pitch sessions what are those pitch sessions so um to do a pitch session I would take your book figure out how to do figure out how to do a one

sentence pitch on it okay you can find out examples of these things online um and whatnot you don't you can't do everything so my one sentence pitch for mistborn was um what if the hero who was supposed to save the world failed and the dark lord took over now a group of Thieves are going to try and Rob him silly uh bribe his armies away overthrow the Empire and make off with the money um you know like it's so it's a mashup of Oceans 11 and Epic Fantasy that's your one sentence pitch does that cover

everything no not at all so you want to have that pitch and then they'll probably say so tell me more and then you are ready with kind of the the longer summary well there's this thief kelsier who was a gentleman Rogue who robbed the rich um for his own benefit he got caught thrown in a terrible prison and in the prison discovered he has incredible magic powers the trauma brought them out he has now escaped and he wants Vengeance on the dark lord the man who years ago thousand years ago took over the world

has been ruling with an iron fist but Kel's way of getting revenge is to rob somebody that's the only thing he knows how to do so he's getting together his old crew he's going to pull off the heist of the Millennia by robbing the dark lord himself problem is he finds out that all their faces are too welln they're all being hunted there are certain things they can't do they need a new recruit so they go and recruit then who's a 16-year-old Street Thief who has been manifesting some of the same powers

that keler has now mastered it's primarily his story and her story is she is taken under his wing trained to use the magic and trained to imitate a noble woman so she can infiltrate Noble Society for them to help them pull off their iist you go into the longer pitch right um kind of hit some of these things but still not hitting plot points um have those two pitches ready practice them in front of your roommates practice them in front of people the one sentence which is really usually like two

sentences put together with semicolons but you know the one sentence pitch and then the longer this is like two or three paragraphs but you want it to go as fast as I just gave mine and then you want to be able to launch into longer descriptions when the person's intrigued of oh that's cool what's the magic oh the Magic's really cool it's like this they're like oh that's great what what happens at the end because they often will want to know that you're like oh it

has a great twist ending they're thinking they're doing this and then this thing happens um and you you just want to be able to talk animatedly about your story like that um if you do what I just did every one of them is going to ask for your sample chapter and then your sample chapters have to be awesome but if your sample chapters are awesome theoretically the last request the full manuscript and what you've got to remember is any of these places that I say take only unagented or take agented

submissions which is you know a lot of these guys if you meet them at a convention they have the right you know they can be like oh yeah send that to me that means you are now you don't have to worry about that whole age of good thing right you can submit to them directly I would still if they then call you and say I'm really interested in this I think we want to make an offer who's your agent that's when you say let me get back to you tomorrow and then you get an agent um or yeah um and there are

very few agents in the business who won't take you if you have someone on this board who is um who has made an offer uh I should mention there are Publishers who are not on this board that are still totally legit um these are kind of the the presses right below Bane in size uh tachon who did the Emperor's soul for me is a very good one that I recommend um py has had some troubles back and forth um but py is another one to look at um that those guys you can research and those are worth being at the problem

is if you go to the rung underneath them then you might as well just be self-publishing generally is my experience right here how do you get an agent overnight how do you get an agent overnight um well theoretically if you've been doing what I've saying you've been submitting to different agents um you at least have a little book of some of the agents who represent the fiction that is like yours that you like um and if you send them emails and say uh you know dear Sharon November um I

just got an offer from uh from Ace for my epic fantasy book They're look they want um they want three of them would you be interested in taking look at it to represent me that's free money right now not every agent will take that well they'll want to see the manuscript because the agent completely hates the manuscript then they'll probably still pass or something like that but every one of them will say yes and if you have a list of like 10 that you can submit to and say I've submitted this to multiple

agents if you could get back to me in the next couple of days um that would be awesome um I need to make a decision pretty soon so we can move forward with this they'll all look at it really fast because again it's free money um and uh someone else has done the work that they normally do and they like that idea okay so you if you have an offer you will not have trouble getting an agent um if you have an agent you can still have trouble getting an offer but it does happen a lot more likely that you will if you get

friends who were reading my fiction and giving me feedback on it what I always did is I finished a book I printed off a copy I had um I I wrote on the cover page here's the type of feedback I'm looking for if you could write this in the margins while you read and pick a pen color and write your name on this cover page in that pen color then I would give it to someone else and I would have 12 people read it um and respond to each other's comments and give me all that that feedback I would

see what I'm doing good doing well and I would say okay this is working this is not working people are really confused here um I am creating the piece of fiction that I want to create I don't know if it's professional caliber but that's okay I mean uh you if at the point where you are you are probably creating stuff that is worth reading it may not be professional caliber but you don't write four books like the worst writers I've known and I've known some pretty bad ones buy about three or four

books they're you know they're good enough right they're good enough that you can enjoy reading the book it may not be good enough to go to the big leagues but they're they're good enough and you might be ready to go to the big leagues so you just have to keep going with this and you have to submit to your friends know that you love it you're never going to know for sure until somebody buys it because you're never going to be able to say well just my you you know it's my friends you're always

got people reading or proof reading your book your manuscript you've got 12 15 friends family whatever say you got 12 you got six people that say this part's confusing and six people that say they totally get it what do you do what do you feel you read that piece yourself and you are the ultimate decision maker um you have to do what is it feels like sometimes you want something to be confusing usually not but once in a while you're like I I want this to be confusion if you want to be confusing

the easiest thing to do is have one of the characters say wow this is confusing um and then that gives the reader permission to be confused they're like oh okay sometimes you're like you know I feel like half of my audience getting confused is too many for this piece it's a really important plot element i' better make this extra clear sometimes you're like ah this is kind of irrelevant I'll actually kind of shove it more under the rug so it doesn't draw their attention and make them think that

this is a major plot point you go all different directions on those things the more you do this the more experience you'll get and if you really want to get some um better feedback you can watch the rewatch the lecture on writing groups and try to take some of those suggestions say try to explain your feelings rather than trying to fix the problem um just write out you know your feelings here or things like that okay other questions yeah how much time do you spend reading other books versus writing

starting idea was for the Stormlight Archive and how did that so the big start of The Stormlight the earliest idea was uh the character of dinar who was the brother to the King after the king gets assassinated whose nephew who should be king is a bad king um and so what do you do if you're put in that position where you love your brother and your nephew it's this whole leadership concept which is fascinating to me right what do you do if you're put in that position do you usurp the throne

for the good of the king or do you let a bad King rule and do your best to keep him from screwing things up too much that was the earliest um idea for The Stormlight Archive um the other big passion thing for me was my belief that Epic Fantasy was not going as far as it needed to go or could go in World building I felt that uh the addition of magic and not having to follow the laws of physics um in consistent ways but you know that sort of thing could lead to really Fantastical interesting settings that

nobody was writing um there was too much medieval Europe or medieval Japan um and I don't mind those books I enjoy the kind of fox medieval whatever but nobody was doing bizarre world um with different ecologies and things like that and I'm like someone should be doing this um and we I should mention that after I made my decision like I had that epiphany of I'm going to do this um I sat down and said I'm going to write the biggest baddest book ever right I am there rejecting me they say my books are

too long I'm going to write one longer right I'm going to write the book that I want to write that no one's ever going to want to publish and that was the way of kings that was book number 13 um that was the book where I'm like no one wants this I will make this book that is only for me and is like the most ambitious thing um and then after I finished it I got the call the luness sold funny story I then sent way of Kings CU my edit mosha he's like I love eless what are you working on now I'm like well it's a

was so scared is that the larger the book gets the less the publisher makes off of it um because like a a CO a book that let's say it's 400,000 words they might charge $2 more than the book that's 200,000 words but the printing cost is double the editorial cost is double everything is double but people don't pay pro-rated in their mind for uh for entertainment a movie that's three hours long costs us in our head the same as one that's 90 minutes long That's just How We Do It um and same thing with

books you can inch it up a little bit more but not much and so it's a huge huge thing for a publisher to try to publish a super big book um by a new author so um but I told him I didn't want to do wave of Kings yet um and he was so relieved because I'm like I'm working on mbor actually right now wave Kings has some problems I want to fix before we publish it so he was very happy to hear I was doing the Because then I pitched mpor to him like the pitch that I just gave you guys he's
